column3: UMich students walk out for International University Day of Action
column7:
The University Insider is The Daily’s first faculty and staff-oriented newsletter. This weekly newsletter will give U-M faculty and staff the ability to see the most important issues on campus and in Ann Arbor — particularly those related to administrative decisions — from the perspective of an independent news organization. It will also provide a better understanding of student perspectives.








Editor’s Note: Mariam Odeh is a former Daily Staffer. Odeh did not contribute to this article.

Correction 11/22: This article has been updated to clarify Haymann’s quote.

Correction 11/24: This article previously misattributed a quote from Odeh to Chowdhury. The article has since been updated to reflect that error.

About 150 University of Michigan students and community members walked out in support of Palestine Thursday afternoon, carrying signs and chanting “Up, up with liberation” and “Down, down with occupation.” The march began on South University Avenue and continued down Tappan and East University Avenues before arriving at Weiser Hall for a teach-in that followed the walk-out. The demonstration was organized by the TAHRIR Coalition, a group of more than 90 pro-Palestine student organizations including Students Allied for Freedom and Equality, the Graduate Employees’ Organization and Jewish Voice for Peace.

This walk-out was in accordance with the National Students for Justice in Palestine’s “International University Day of Action,” a global initiative urging universities to divest from companies profiting off Israel’s ongoing military campaign in Gaza.

Before the march, several speakers addressed the crowd, expressing demands for divestment and solidarity with the Palestinian people. The march took place entirely on public roads in order to avoid violating the University’s Disruptive Activity Policy, which restricts protests from interfering with campus events and operations. 

SAFE held other programming throughout the day,including screen printing, glass cutting and fabric printing.

Engineering senior Mariam Odeh, a member of the coalition, said the march concluded with a teach-in at the Weiser Hall to educate participants on the movement for divestment. 

“The premise was we leave class, we gather together, we protest the University’s complicity in the genocide, but we also learn about it, hence the teach-in right now,” Odeh said. “As we say divestment, we mean a lot of things, but people don’t really understand what that means. So this event is going into the details of what the U-M endowment is, what U-M is invested in and when we say divestment, what we want them to take their investments out of.”

Attendees were barred from entering the Weiser Hall for approximately 15 minutes by three officers from the U-M Department of Public Safety and Security. The room where the teach-in was meant to take place was booked by a GEO member, but the University asked to see the room reservations multiple times, according to LSA senior Alifa Chowdhury, CSG president. 

“We went through all the standards and procedures that you’re supposed to,” Odeh said. “(The DPSS officers) refused to let us into the building, saying ‘We need to see the reservation.’ And we showed them the reservation, and they were still being hesitant,” Odeh said. “I think it says a lot about how uncomfortable they are with students learning about divestment, but also gathering together in person.”

Pro-Palestine student activists have alleged institutional suppression of pro-Palestine advocacy on campus. This includes criticism of the aforementioned Disruptive Activity Policy in addition to recent updates to the Statement of Student Rights and Responsibilities. The revisions allow the University to act as a complainant in misconduct cases and have been criticized for undermining shared governance and free speech. The University has since filed a complaint against SAFE with the possibility of a suspension of the organization.

Odeh criticized the University and the Board of Regents for what she called a failure to support students advocating for Palestinian rights. She said her impeachment by CSG, the complaint against SAFE filed by the University and other actions against student activism were part of a broader attempt to stifle dissent.

“I think the University is trying to do everything they can to suppress our movement, but they don’t realize we’re doing everything we can to continue, so they can’t silence us,” Odeh said. 

Business junior Izzie Haymann, director of strategy and event planning for the University’s chapter of Students Supporting Israel, attended the event with several other students in hopes of engaging in conversation with the protesters. 

“What we’re doing here today is really just standing there observing in hopes of having good, productive academic discourse, and also just making sure that none of the campus climate is exacerbated and there’s no disruptions occurring on campus.” Haymann said. 

Chowdhury is currently in the process of being impeached by the Assembly. She said the TAHRIR coalition remains undeterred despite the challenges posed by University policies and actions, citing disciplinary actions taken against activists involved in previous protests.

“We will be here,” Chowdhury said. “(This) just kind of goes hand in hand with everything else the University has been doing, so instituting discipline and targeting participants in the Honors convocation disruption, due process, submitting a complaint against or threatening two to four year suspension, hiring an outside consultant to bring the case against us.”

column3: Institute for the Humanities hosts Ericka Lopez exhibit
column7:
About 20 people gathered at the Institute for the Humanities Wednesday evening to participate in the “Lost and Newfound: Exploring the Art of Ericka Lopez,” an event featuring an interactive crafting session and a curator-led tour of Lopez’s exhibit, “touch,” which is currently on display at the institute.

Lopez is a blind and nonverbal artist that makes ceramic pots and punch rugs based on her memories of colors and textures. At her exhibit, visitors are allowed to touch and interact with the artwork.

As the event began, attendees were offered the opportunity to go on a tour to view and touch Lopez’s artwork, which was on exhibition  in a room next to the event. The tour was led by the institute’s curator, Amanda Krugliak, who brought Lopez’s work to the University of Michigan after first discovering it in a Chinatown gallery near Los Angeles. Krugliak told The Michigan Daily that she felt moved by the authenticity of Lopez’s work. 

“I think there may be a whole way of experiencing artwork through touch that we haven’t even really tapped into,” Krugliak said. “There’s something about the hand of the artist in your hand and the transfer of the work where you can imagine the hand that made it.”

It is rare that viewers are able to physically engage with work in a gallery or museum setting because there are often concerns about damage. However, the aspect of touch and physical engagement is central to Lopez’s artwork because it represents how she interacts with her own art, according to Krugliak. 

“She’s really involved with each of her pieces,” Krugliak said. “She hugs them. She greets them. After many weeks of being away from the work, when she arrived, she knew exactly what the pieces were based on touch. I felt like the celebration of her work while she was here and the attention received for this work was really important to her and to all of us.”

In preparation for Lopez’s visit, Krugliak and Gallery Coordinator Ed Diven spent months coming up with a vision for the exhibit. The ceramic pots were displayed on moon-shaped tables, which were chosen to be both futuristic and wheelchair accessible. Additionally, Diven met with disability consultants for guidance on Braille placement, QR codes with links to website information for accessibility and shims along the baseboards to aid those requiring a cane to walk.

Information and LSA senior Claude VanValkenburg and LSA senior Lucian Van Fleet are student interns for the Institute for the Humanities and were the organizers of the event. Van Fleet explained that each intern organizes one event per semester. The pair chose Lopez’s exhibit because they love the idea of art bringing people together. 

“Both of us are very artistically inclined,” Van Fleet said. “We love doing arts and crafts, so we decided to do this because we wanted to pay homage to the art, because it’s so incredibly beautiful, but also do it in a way that would bring people together and kind of have fun and enjoy the art as a group.”

A punch rug activity, which began with a tutorial, accompanied the exhibit. Participants were given the opportunity to use yarn to make their punch rugs, and also to attach different kinds of 3D objects to their rugs, emulating Lopez’s art practice.

LSA senior Delia Binetti said the process of making a colorful rug helped her decompress.

“I think what I took away is that it’s very important to give ourselves time to … take a step back from school,” Binetti said. “I feel like sometimes I get wrapped up in just needing to finish assignments or study, when sometimes you just need to do activities like this, and meanwhile, while doing it, I just learned about a super cool artist.”





For the next month, the University of Michigan Institute for the Humanities will feature the work of Jewish lesbian artist Phranc, whose exhibit “The Butch Closet” features posters, magazines, memorabilia and clothes made out of cardboard recreate Phranc’s childhood closet.  The multi-media exhibit, which opened Thursday, details Phranc’s life as…

“Silver Linings,” a traveling exhibit of 40 works from the Spelman College Museum of Fine Art's collection, debuted at the University of Michigan Museum of Art on Aug. 24. The exhibit, which will remain at UMMA through Jan. 5, celebrates Black art production and the commitment to collecting art by…

The University of Michigan Arts Initiative and the Ross Impact Studio began hosting “Making it: $napshots from the Artist Pay Project,” an anonymous journalism series that uses artists’ records and finance journals to explore how they navigate financial health, on Nov. 15. The exhibit features interviews with more than 30…

column3: Student Life VP Martino Harmon talks campus climate, sustainability and student activism in exclusive interview
column7:
The Michigan Daily sat down with Martino Harmon, the University of Michigan’s vice president for student life, to discuss how voting, student protests and bias-motivated incidents have affected the U-M community this past semester.

This interview has been edited and condensed for clarity.

The Michigan Daily: Ann Arbor saw a high early voter turnout in this year’s general election, marking the first presidential election with in-person early voting. How did the University of Michigan support student voting this year compared to the 2022 midterm election’s long lines on Election Day?

Martino Harmon: We’re excited that students got engaged in the democratic process, and that was really the result of a coalition called UMICH Votes. Then there were key members of that coalition, some of which would be the Ginsberg Center of Student Life, Turn Up Turnout, the City of Ann Arbor and our academic partners as well. So really it’s everyone working together and putting the message out about opportunities for students to get involved, whether it be through social media, nonpartisan messages, messages about registering early so students don’t register the day of and then even fun things, like a video I was able to participate in with some students that I know as well as Vice President for Government Relations Chris Kolb. I really believe that all those communications, along with the access through the two campus hubs at the University of Michigan Museum of Art and the Duderstadt provided students with the opportunity to vote. From what I understand, over 15,000 votes were cast through the two campus voting hubs during this election. All in all, it was a team effort, and students had great opportunities to participate.

TMD: Students have continued to protest Israel’s military campaign in Gaza throughout the semester and asked the University to divest from companies profiting off the Israeli military campaign in Gaza. How is Student Life ensuring that student activists feel acknowledged and heard?

MH: We have a long history at the University of activism. The University is the marketplace of ideas, and demonstrations are one way to really make change at universities. We absolutely support that, but it’s also important for us in Student Life to work proactively with our partners — whether it’s the Division of Public Safety & Security or other partners across campus — to not only ensure activism but to make sure that students’ events can go as planned, to make sure that they don’t have disruption of their events, to make sure they know their rights, but also their responsibilities in terms of demonstrations and to make sure the demonstrations are held safely. 

It’s important for us to proactively work with students, but we’re also there during events, just to make sure everything is going as planned, or if students have questions or concerns during the event, they know who to turn to. What’s critical, though, in the planning, is to make sure that student groups understand time, place and manner restrictions. We’re a public institution, and the right to free speech is important, but there are time, place and manner restrictions, and it’s really important that students know about that. We are actively engaged to make sure they follow those. The work that we do is really important to ensure their rights are upheld and that they have safe demonstrations.

TMD: Alterations to the Student Statement of Rights and Responsibilities have been criticized for potentially creating fear of protesting in students. What is your response to these concerns?

MH: In addition to all the things that we do to support activism, it’s also really important that we provide clarity, and that’s what the Statement of Student Rights and Responsibilities does. It provides clarity in terms of students’ understanding of time, place and manner restrictions, what some of the limits are so that they don’t really go beyond that, and we really owe it to the community to have a safe academic environment where all students can learn in a safe way. Individuals, of course, have the right to protest as long as those protests don’t infringe on the rights of others. It’s about understanding your rights but also understanding your responsibilities and then knowing that there are consequences to the actions that violate policy or law. It’s really important for us to keep that document up to date and make sure that students know what their rights are. 

TMD: Multiple assaults against Jewish individuals have been reported on campus this semester, which University President Santa Ono has condemned. How is Student Life ensuring students feel safe on campus and supported in their identities?

MH: That is such an important issue; those incidents are horrible. No person should be targeted based on their identity, and that goes for all identities, not just the ones referenced in the question. One of my biggest priorities is to make sure students feel a sense of belonging. A sense of belonging means feeling safe, that you can go about campus and have an experience without feeling like you’re targeted or concerned about your own safety. We have to avoid creating a hostile environment at the University. That’s really important, and that’s related to race, color, national origin. 

The entire division of Student Life works to make sure the students are supported. We have a Campus Climate Support unit within the Dean of Students, and they partner with the Equity, Civil Rights & Title IX Office. They also partner with the Office of Diversity, Equity & Inclusion and DPSS, so that when incidents do arise, they make sure there is a system of reporting, but also a system of support for those individuals that may be affected by those incidents. We try to really engage with students, to listen and learn. When there is an incident, we don’t just get the report, but we also want to learn from that incident so that we can hopefully avoid future incidents of that nature.

TMD: The Board of Regents recently approved an institutional neutrality bylaw. How will this affect campus climate for students?

MH: I don’t think it’ll have a direct impact on campus climate. What the institutional neutrality law is meant to do is to make sure that institutional leaders at certain levels — the president, vice presidents and deans — refrain from making statements that aren’t directly related to the campus itself. This allows for other individuals to have their own viewpoints so that it doesn’t feel like the top administrators are setting the viewpoints based on statements that we may make. We want to also encourage freedom of expression, but we just really want to be more disciplined in our approach and when we do make statements, making sure that it is related to a particular campus issue or campus concern.

TMD: The University is now in the second year of the DEI 2.0 strategic plan, recently criticized in The New York Times. Does Student Life plan to take these criticisms into consideration when implementing its DEI goals? Why or why not?

MH: Student life along with the University really should take the critique from the New York Times and assess it, to determine what are areas we need to look at as opportunities for actionable improvement. But also, recognizing that DEI is one of our core values, and it’s something that we’re always in a constant improvement mode. I think we recognize that DEI is complex, it’s challenging. I think another view is Dr. Tabbye Chavous’ response to the article, which is another piece of information that we should take into account when reviewing the article and the critique that was set forth in that.

TMD: Multiple events this semester have brought students and experts together to discuss climate and sustainability. How will Student Life continue to prioritize sustainability and aligning with the University’s carbon neutrality goals this year?

MH: Our really special role is to primarily support the student organizations that are involved in sustainability work, such as the Student Sustainability Coalition. We have a small staff that works with them and student employees to help promote their activities. On a personal level, I really enjoyed participating in Earthfest this year. I actually helped plant a tree, which is something I haven’t done in probably 25 years. I visited the Farm Stand a couple times this year and made some purchases there. 

We also have key capital projects that are part of the University sustainability goals. For example, the new Edward and Rosalie Ginsburg Building, which will open sometime before the fall of 2025, was really our first project that focused on sustainability. The Hadley Family Recreation & Well-Being Center includes a lot of natural sunlight, as well as other parts of the construction that are geared towards sustainability. The Central Campus residential development, the 2,300-bed housing facility, is also a major sustainability project. 

With the new projects, we’re working within the University sustainability goals, but we will also continue to look for opportunities in the renovation of existing buildings to make them more sustainable and respond to concerns about climate change. On a personal level, it’s an opportunity of learning and growth for me, and getting involved hands-on with some of the things going on is exciting.

TMD: In light of the upcoming holiday, what is your favorite food item on your Thanksgiving meal plate? 

MH: Oh, my goodness. A lot of choices. It is probably the simplest item that involves no preparation whatsoever but I cannot eat enough of it: It’s the cranberry sauce. It’s not even one that’s made but right out of the can, drop it and slice it. I never eat it anytime other than Thanksgiving, but I love it. I love all the other Thanksgiving foods, but if someone eats all the cranberry sauce and I don’t get any, there’s going to be a real problem.

They also partner with the Office of Diversity, Equity & Inclusion and <PERSON>, so that when incidents do arise, they make sure there is a system of reporting, but also a system of support for those individuals that may be affected by those incidents. We try to really engage with students, to listen and learn. When there is an incident, we don’t just get the report, but we also want to learn from that incident so that we can hopefully avoid future incidents of that nature.

TMD: The Board of Regents recently approved an institutional neutrality bylaw. How will this affect campus climate for students?

MH: I don’t think it’ll have a direct impact on campus climate. What the institutional neutrality law is meant to do is to make sure that institutional leaders at certain levels — the president, vice presidents and deans — refrain from making statements that aren’t directly related to the campus itself. This allows for other individuals to have their own viewpoints so that it doesn’t feel like the top administrators are setting the viewpoints based on statements that we may make. We want to also encourage freedom of expression, but we just really want to be more disciplined in our approach and when we do make statements, making sure that it is related to a particular campus issue or campus concern.

TMD: The University is now in the second year of the DEI 2.0 strategic plan, recently criticized in The New York Times. Does Student Life plan to take these criticisms into consideration when implementing its DEI goals? Why or why not?

MH: Student life along with the University really should take the critique from the New York Times and assess it, to determine what are areas we need to look at as opportunities for actionable improvement. But also, recognizing that DEI is one of our core values, and it’s something that we’re always in a constant improvement mode. I think we recognize that DEI is complex, it’s challenging. I think another view is Dr. <PERSON>’ response to the article, which is another piece of information that we should take into account when reviewing the article and the critique that was set forth in that.

TMD: Multiple events this semester have brought students and experts together to discuss climate and sustainability. How will Student Life continue to prioritize sustainability and aligning with the University’s carbon neutrality goals this year?

MH: Our really special role is to primarily support the student organizations that are involved in sustainability work, such as the Student Sustainability Coalition. We have a small staff that works with them and student employees to help promote their activities. On a personal level, I really enjoyed participating in Earthfest this year. I actually helped plant a tree, which is something I haven’t done in probably 25 years. I visited the Farm Stand a couple times this year and made some purchases there. 

We also have key capital projects that are part of the University sustainability goals. For example, the new <PERSON> and <PERSON>, which will open sometime before the fall of 2025, was really our first project that focused on sustainability. The Hadley Family Recreation & Well-Being Center includes a lot of natural sunlight, as well as other parts of the construction that are geared towards sustainability. The Central Campus residential development, the 2,300-bed housing facility, is also a major sustainability project. 

With the new projects, we’re working within the University sustainability goals, but we will also continue to look for opportunities in the renovation of existing buildings to make them more sustainable and respond to concerns about climate change. On a personal level, it’s an opportunity of learning and growth for me, and getting involved hands-on with some of the things going on is exciting.

TMD: In light of the upcoming holiday, what is your favorite food item on your Thanksgiving meal plate? 

MH: Oh, my goodness. A lot of choices. It is probably the simplest item that involves no preparation whatsoever but I cannot eat enough of it: It’s the cranberry sauce. It’s not even one that’s made but right out of the can, drop it and slice it. I never eat it anytime other than Thanksgiving, but I love it. I love all the other Thanksgiving foods, but if someone eats all the cranberry sauce and I don’t get any, there’s going to be a real problem.

column3: Judith Butler talks academic freedom and democracy
column7:
The University Insider is The Daily’s first faculty and staff-oriented newsletter. This weekly newsletter will give U-M faculty and staff the ability to see the most important issues on campus and in Ann Arbor — particularly those related to administrative decisions — from the perspective of an independent news organization. It will also provide a better understanding of student perspectives.








University of Michigan Faculty Senate hosted philosopher Judith Butler at Hutchins Hall Thursday evening for their annual Davis, Markert, and Nickerson Academic Freedom Lecture. The lecture took on a new weight in the context of current debates on campus regarding the University’s divestment from companies profiting off Israel’s military campaign in Gaza and the results of the recent presidential election. 

Butler, most famous for their 1990 book, “Gender Trouble: Feminism and the Subversion of Identity,” drew a packed crowd of more than 200 U-M community members. Their lecture, titled “Academic Freedom in a Time of Destruction,” focused on extramural speech — speech as a citizen outside of the academic institution — and the intertwined nature of democracy and academic freedom.

Gayle Rubin, professor of anthropology and women’s studies, introduced Butler, connecting their work on gender theory, which has drawn backlash from conservatives, to the debate on transgender issues during the presidential election. Rubin specifically mentioned Trump’s comments on gender-affirming care during the presidential debate.

“Being in a swing state, I got so many robocalls telling me that Kamala Harris was spending taxpayer funds to get sex change surgeries for illegal immigrants,” Rubin said. “It was wild. So, attacks against LGBTQ populations and institutions are, once again, part of a broader antidemocratic oligarchy, male supremacist and racist mobilization, and that’s one of many reasons why Professor Butler is such an appropriate person for this year’s lecture.”

Butler also joined a discussion panel on their new book, “Who’s Afraid of Gender?” at the Ford School of Public Policy Wednesday afternoon. Rubin praised Butler for authoring the book while faced with right-wing attacks on gender ideology. 

“Judith Butler has become such a lightning rod, often attacked by people who have no idea of the actual contents of their work,” Rubin said. “Under such conditions, it takes unusual fortitude, determination and, frankly, sheer guts, to continue to bear witness to one’s ideas, one’s opinions and one’s convictions.”

Butler started their lecture by acknowledging the recent election, urging the audience to consider the consequences Trump’s administration poses to immigrant rights, constitutional rights and the future of democracy. 

“The American people have, by a clear majority, voted against democracy, but they have done this through an electoral process that is part of democracy, even indispensable to its operation,” Butler said. “And yet, the people can and have opposed democracy exercising their popular will in order to elect someone who has promised to destroy the balance of powers, to initiate mass deportation and to undo constitutional protections, step by step.” 

In light of the election results, Butler encouraged a shift in focus towards community organizing and solidarity.

“When the shock is done, we will, I think, look around and ask, what are the communities we might serve?” Butler said. “We can, in this electoral aftermath, despair of democracy, or we can seek to renew it in a new form.” 

Butler spent the majority of the lecture discussing the blurred line between extramural and intramural speech. Academic freedom encompasses the right to both intramural speech, which concerns university matters, and extramural speech, when a professor is speaking as a citizen in a context outside of the institution. Butler referenced the etymology of the word extramural, which means “outside the wall,” saying the word itself is ambiguous. 

“What constitutes the ‘wall’ within the contemporary university or college?” Butler said. “Does the wall designate space outside the classroom but still on the campus, or does it designate space outside the campus in a generalized public sphere, or specific jurisdictions adjacent to universities or including them? Where’s the wall in the space of the virtual classroom?” 

Butler also criticized the University for using police force to shut down the Gaza solidarity encampment in the name of campus security. The TAHRIR Coalition, which organized the encampment, did so as part of a call for the University’s Board of Regents to divest from companies profiting from Israel’s military campaign in Gaza. According to Butler, clearing the encampment opposed the principles of academic freedom of extramural speech.

“Can we understand the permission that so many administrators have given themselves to unleash … police powers on those seeking to exercise rights of speech and assembly on campus as an example of a rationality that substitutes risk management, the protection of private property and the demands for hyper security, for a commitment to academic freedom, including the protection of extramural speech?” Butler said.

Finally, Butler spoke on the distinction between anti-Zionism — opposition to the state of Israel — and antisemitism. As a Jewish person, they said they understand when Jewish students say they feel unsafe when Zionism is criticized. However, Butler said their point of view was challenged during their time attending a university with an open sphere of debate. 

“I started to read — to read lots of books, some of them by Israeli historians, some by Palestinian scholars, poets, novelists,” Butler said. “When I read and read, my views changed slowly over time. But I actually understand the students who say ‘I feel unsafe.’ I understand. But you know what? Sometimes the safety you take in a point of view that has become the consensus, when that consensus is now being challenged, we have to be rattled. We have to be disturbed.” 

LSA and Art & Design fifth-year student Noe Conahan said in an interview with The Michigan Daily that she attended the event because, as a women’s and gender studies major, she has read and admires Butler’s work.

“It’s really cool to be able to hear them speak, because they’ve been so foundational to feminist theory and Queer theory,” Conahan said. “It definitely got me to think more about academic free speech as an important kind of pillar of not just democracy, but all kinds of social justice organizing.”

column3: Ford School hosts PeaceWorks talk with Israeli and Palestinian experts
column7:
The Ford School of Public Policy held a roundtable discussion with Israeli and Palestinian peacemakers working to end Israel’s military campaign in Gaza Tuesday afternoon. The event, titled “Agents of Peace: Ground-Level Insights from Israeli and Palestinian Peacemakers,” was hosted in collaboration with the PeaceWorks Foundation.

The PeaceWorks Foundation promotes intergroup dialogue between Israelis and Palestinians. This event was specifically hosted by PeaceWorks on Campus, a partner of the PeaceWorks Foundation that aims to integrate college students’ voices into its discussions. 

Nidal Foqaha, director general of the Palestinian Peace Coalition, and Tehila Wenger, deputy director of the Geneva Initiative, headlined the conversation. Foqaha heads a Palestinian nonprofit to promote a two-state solution as outlined by the Geneva Initiative. Wenger works primarily with the Geneva Initiative, a nongovernmental organization that uses education and discussion to promote peace negotiations between Israelis and Palestinians.

Wenger said her experiences visiting different universities have been eye-opening. While she originally expected a low level of participation, she was surprised at how many diverse opinions students have shared.

“Something I’ve heard over and over again on different campuses is this atmosphere of fear, fear of saying the wrong thing or of being perceived as being on one side or the other,” Wenger said. “I’m scared of people being scared to speak, or to ask the right questions or the wrong questions because our whole premise is that we will only move forward if we talk.”

Wenger and Foqaha answered questions from students and faculty about the challenges and opportunities of working towards a policy solution in Israel and Palestine. Foqaha emphasized that he believes conversation is the best way to bridge the gap between the two groups.

“We firmly believe in talking and dialogue as the only way for solving conflicts,” Foqaha said. “Instead of even waiting for any round of violence to happen and then as a result to initiate dialogue between the two sides, this should be the starting point.”

Both speakers believe a two-state goal is the most viable solution, and noted that they believe the two nations have a mutual interest in ending the ongoing violence in the region.

“I promise you, no matter what you’re hearing in the media on both sides, those pragmatic voices that just want the killing to end and just want their basic rights do exist,” Wenger said. “That’s the predominance in both societies.”

Foqaha argued that the involvement of Hamas in a potential two-state goal may influence the international support of the plan. 

“We depend, or we will depend in the future when it comes to rebuilding Gaza, on the political and financial backing of our brothers in the Gulf (Cooperation Council) … or how about the world will be depending on the United States,” Foqaha said. “We do not have the least expectation that all these such countries would accept actually supporting an administration where Hamas is playing the role.”

The two discussed the role of foreign intervention in the Israeli military campaign following Donald Trump’s recent victory in the United States presidential election. Wenger argued that Trump presents himself as strongly pro-Israel and anti-Palestine, which gives him some degree of influence over Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.

“I feel a little more optimistic in terms of a cease-fire in Gaza under a Trump administration than under a Harris administration, simply because I think that Trump has leverage with Netanyahu that Harris does not,” Wenger said.

Foqaha used the term, “The Day After,” to describe what might happen if the Israeli military campaign in Gaza comes to an end, and how negotiation now would be necessary to maintain stability. 

“When it comes to ‘The Day After’, it’s extremely important to combine diplomatic efforts towards promoting a window of political opportunity between the Israelis and Palestinians on one hand, but also to focus on allowing the relatively convenient conditions for the Gazans to stay and to remain in their homes and continue their lives,” Foqaha said.

Public Policy junior Alanah Dakar told The Michigan Daily she attended the event to learn more about the perspectives of other University of Michigan students.

“I came to hear the opinions of my peers and the questions they thought were important to ask,” Dakar said. “I’m familiar with the PeaceWorks organization and their main goals, but we’re here with the Ford community and the Michigan student body who are pursuing politics as a career … as the future leaders of American policy.”

column3: UMich Table Tennis Club petitions for University support
column7:
The Michigan Table Tennis Club ended a successful season last spring, placing third in the region and seventh nationally. Despite these accolades, the team is not able to practice on campus or wear the University of Michigan’s logo on their jerseys after losing University recognition in 2017. Now, they are circulating a petition asking the University to guarantee funding and practice space on campus.  

The team was sponsored by the University up until 2017, when a dispute between the University’s Department of Recreational Sports and the team over travel form deadlines culminated in the termination of funding. LSA senior Alex Cai, a co-president of the team, told The Michigan Daily in an interview about the events leading up to losing U-M funding.

“The general gist is that the team (in 2017) basically made it to Nationals, but the school denied their request to travel to compete,” Cai said. “And (the team) basically went without telling them and the school found out. … Everything else that happened after was a result of that.”

In an interview with The Daily, LSA senior Adham Musa, the team’s treasurer, said the current team is not affiliated with the incident in 2017, but still faces its repercussions.  

“We want to emphasize that the 2017 incident was in regards to travel forms, it had nothing to do with us,” Musa said. “It was all previous players and most players that graduated that year. Over the other years, other board members tried to reach out to the (Board of Regents) with no clear response of how to proceed in terms of how to get university sponsorship.”

Since 2017, the team has been limited to practicing off-campus at local community centers, including the Jewish Community Center of Greater Ann Arbor and the Muslim Community Association of Ann Arbor. Relying mainly on these centers, as well as contributions from local businesses and community members, the team has been able to obtain practice space as well as equipment and funding to continue to operate and compete. 

The team is currently collecting and finalizing signatures on their formal petition to the University in which they detail their immediate and long-term goals. With more than 100 signatures from U-M faculty, students and community members, the petition lays out the team’s three requests. First, the team is requesting a one-time grant for 12 new NCTTA-approved table tennis tables and nets. Second, the team requests a guaranteed practice time and space at the Hadley Family Recreation & Wellbeing Center, which is currently set to open in spring 2025. The team’s last request asks the Center for Campus Involvement to endorse the club, which would allow the use of the U-M logo on their jerseys. 

Musa told The Daily he feels that on-campus practice spaces and University support could substantially benefit the team. 

“There is definitely a question of how practical it is for the team to practice (at the community centers) because of space constraints,” Musa said. “It is without question that the spaces on campus, especially the new buildings they’re building, could provide us a much better place and could be much more convenient for the team to better prepare.”

LSA senior Nehemiah Yu, the team’s co-president, told The Daily the lack of practice space and adequate equipment has also been discouraging, especially as the team is made up of accomplished players. 

“The quality of the practice and training is extremely important,” Yu said. “We’re hosting an international Olympian, and it doesn’t feel great to tell her that we’re not officially sponsored by the school.” 

This year, the table tennis team welcomed 2024 Olympian Archana Kamath, who represented India on their women’s table tennis team. The team’s roster also includes Eliel Sosis, who was on the USA U19 Boy’s Junior National Team, and Yijia Gao, who competed in the 2022 College Table Tennis National Championships. 

Cai also said it is difficult to not be able to officially be supported by the University, especially when competing with teams from other universities who are recognized by their institutions. 

“It’s just not great to say that we’re not even officially sponsored by the school,” Cai said. “Especially when basically all the other big universities like UCLA, NYU, (UC) Berkeley … get sponsorship and money and they’re recognized as — at the very minimum — a club sport.”

Musa said the team’s ultimate goal in circulating the petition is to receive official recognition and assistance from the University to build stability and improve performance. 

“Since we play in Michigan’s name, we should be more supported by the University,” Musa said. “Basically, we see that we’ve done very good without (the University’s) support, and we think we could do a lot better if we do get their support.”

<PERSON> said the team’s ultimate goal in circulating the petition is to receive official recognition and assistance from the University to build stability and improve performance. 

“Since we play in Michigan’s name, we should be more supported by the University,” <PERSON> said. “Basically, we see that we’ve done very good without (the University’s) support, and we think we could do a lot better if we do get their support.”

column3: ‘One Year Since’ exhibition and memorial service honors lives lost in Hamas’ Oct. 7 attack on Israel
column7:
Michigan Hillel hosted a day-long exhibition and memorial service on the Diag Monday to mark the one year anniversary of Hamas’ Oct. 7 attack on Israel. The exhibition aimed to memorialize those killed by Hamas since the Oct. 7 attack and call attention to the 101 hostages still being held in Gaza. Throughout the day, the exhibition displayed photographs taken of Israel before and after the attack, posters of missing and dead Israeli and American citizens on large cardboard boxes made to look like milk cartons and Israeli flags in the shape of the star of David on the Ingalls Mall knoll. 

More than 400 U-M students and community members gathered for the memorial service following the exhibition, which featured speeches from U-M students, rabbis, Israeli visitors Noa Reuveni and Shay Raz and Jewish community members. 

The memorial was opened by Marla Linderman Richelew, president of the Jewish Federation of Greater Ann Arbor. She welcomed the crowd to the service and called for remembrance and compassion.

“We gather together this evening — students, faculty, staff, alumni, parents, local citizens, people of all faiths and ethnicities — to remember, to comfort, to bear witness and to pray for healing and peace and to hope for a better future,” Richelew said. “Commemorating the significant and tragic event calls us together for consolation, unity and strength.”

Richelew was followed by LSA senior Ryan Silberfein, Michigan Hillel president, who emphasized the importance of honoring those killed throughout Israel’s military campaign in Gaza, not just on Oct. 7.

“While we’re here to mark the unprovoked terror attack (on) Oct. 7 against Israel and that is the primary focus of this gathering, we hold in our hearts all of the innocent civilians in Gaza and now Lebanon who have been killed or injured in these past 12 months,” Silberfein said. “In the coming year, may our communities learn to hold space for and embrace one another.”

After the two opening speakers, speeches were interspersed with other forms of remembrance such as videos of Israel and Israeli citizens, poems, Hebrew music and prayers. 

One such prayer was led by Rabbi Nadav Caine from the Beth Israel Congregation, who read a prayer for the state of Israel. Caine explained the significance of the prayer in the context of the day.

“We pray for the state of Israel in this devastating time of war, of shock and of deep grief,” Caine said. “Our hearts are breaking. We pray for the lives of the hostages captured by Hamas, the civilians and the soldiers. Watch over them, shelter them, bring them home. We pray for the souls of the innocent victims who were brutally murdered. … We pray for our family in Israel in this time of crisis and tragedy.”

LSA junior Ryan Finlay told The Michigan Daily that, as a Jewish student, he felt attending Monday’s event was an important way to commemorate the events of Oct. 7, 2023.

“I had to come out today in order to remember Oct. 7 because this truly is a monumental day for the Jewish people in the worst way,” Finlay said. “Today, there’s no place I would rather be than outside on the Diag, helping to remember the atrocity that happened one year ago and to help unite the Jewish people on campus.”

Finlay said that since Oct. 7, 2023, he has been increasingly aware of his identity as a Jewish student, describing a shared sense of loss experienced by the Jewish community, as well as changes in his interactions with his peers. 

“I had never really thought much of being a Jewish student on campus before Oct. 7 last year, and then everything changed after Oct. 7, because first of all, the Jewish community came together like never before,” Finlay said. “We felt this shared sense of loss, and then we felt this shared sense that we were being opposed by so many people who didn’t care about our suffering. … The people that we used to know, people we used to have as friends, people that we’ve been going to class with, look at us in a totally different way.” 

The final speaker, LSA senior Evan Cohen, president of Wolverine for Israel, called for hope despite the tragedy of the circumstances.

“In the darkness, we find glimpses of light,” Cohen said. “We see joy when hostages are rescued through seemingly impossible missions, we see strength as Israelis come together to support each other and we see unity at home, as the Jewish community and our allies fight for what’s right.”

column3: TAHRIR Coalition organizes walkout calling attention to one-year mark of Israel’s military campaign in Gaza
column7:
The University Insider is The Daily’s first faculty and staff-oriented newsletter. This weekly newsletter will give U-M faculty and staff the ability to see the most important issues on campus and in Ann Arbor — particularly those related to administrative decisions — from the perspective of an independent news organization. It will also provide a better understanding of student perspectives.








About 250 University of Michigan students walked out of their classes at 3:30 p.m. Monday and gathered outside Rackham Graduate School to prepare for a march on campus organized by the TAHRIR Coalition, a group of more than 90 pro-Palestine organizations. At approximately 5:08 p.m. outside the Kinesiology Building, an altercation broke out, leading U-M Division of Public Safety & Security officers to arrest one individual. Students preventing the passage of the vehicle transporting the arrested individual were pushed aside and some were pepper sprayed by DPSS officers. 

Engineering senior Jenin Alameddin, SAFE member, spoke to the crowd outside Rackham and said that Monday’s demonstration was held to recognize the last year of Palestinian resistance and pay tribute to the Palestinians killed by Israel’s military campaign in Gaza.

“We do not just stand here to mourn the dead,” Alameddin said. “We stand here to honor the resistance. On Oct. 7, 2023, Palestinian resistance fighters launched a powerful attack on Israeli military positions, the fighters broke through the heavily fortified barriers surrounding Gaza, showing that no wall, no matter how high or how strong, can suppress the will of a people fighting for their homeland.” 

LSA senior Alifa Chowdhury, Central Student Government president, spoke at the walkout and said she believes it is important for U-M students to take action to help Palestinians. 

“We must recognize our own immense privilege without drones or bombs over our heads,” Chowdhury said. “We must learn from the people of Palestine what it means for education to be a responsibility. Our responsibility is clear. Our university is complicit in sending billions of dollars to a genocide. We are complicit through our silence and privilege.”

Chowdhury and members of the SHUT IT DOWN party recently proposed a CSG budget plan that would only allocate funds toward essential support resources for low-income students and contribute the remaining budget to rebuilding schools in Gaza that have been destroyed by the Israeli military campaign.

Art & Design senior Eaman Ali, a member of the TAHRIR Coalition, told the crowd about the encampment raid that took place on May 21, during which police forcibly removed the encampment, arresting four protesters and pepper spraying others. 

“The UMich Gaza solidarity encampment stood for a month this past spring when on May 21 it was violently raided by police in riot gear at the request of administration,” Ali said. 

As the speeches concluded, the students took to the streets and began their march. After the arrest, they rerouted the march to the DPSS station to demand the individual’s release.

U-M alum Salma Hamamy, former Students Allied for Freedom and Equality president, told The Michigan Daily the arrest was sudden and confusing to many individuals.

“Once we crossed in front of the Kinesiology Building, we began to notice that police officers handcuffed and arrested one of our comrades in the moment, and everybody was pretty confused as to why,” Hamamy said. “Nobody could really understand as to why the police arrested this individual. It was very sudden, this person was just chanting just as much as anybody else.”

Hamamy said the protesters began demanding the release of the individual when the police pepper sprayed students and pushed them around. 

“Protesters jumped in and demanded his immediate release, and the police continued to push through, brutalizing students using pepper spray, pepper spraying dozens of students in the process and community members, leaving many people quite traumatized — however, not in fear to come and continue protesting,” Hamamy said. “Everybody marched over immediately to the DPSS police station, and upon arrival, our friend was released.” 

In an email to The Daily, Melissa Overton, deputy chief of police for DPSS, wrote that DPSS was still investigating the situation and officers can take precautions they deem necessary when handling a potentially dangerous situation. 

“This is an active investigation with details still being confirmed, however, it’s important to note that in some circumstances, officers may employ crowd control measures if deemed necessary to manage the situation, make arrests, or ensure the safety of individuals involved,” Overton wrote.

In an interview with The Daily, Ali said she believes the arrest shows the administration is afraid of what the student body can do. 

“They released our friend who was arrested within the hour because we came here, we showed up and we made our presence known,” Ali said. “The administration is very clearly resorting to these tactics because they are afraid of our power, and they’re afraid of the power that we leverage as a student body, as a university.”

column3: Pro-Palestine supporters construct mock border wall on Diag
column7:
The University Insider is The Daily’s first faculty and staff-oriented newsletter. This weekly newsletter will give U-M faculty and staff the ability to see the most important issues on campus and in Ann Arbor — particularly those related to administrative decisions — from the perspective of an independent news organization. It will also provide a better understanding of student perspectives.








Nearly two decades after their first annual demonstration in 2003, members of Students Allied for Freedom and Equality at the University of Michigan constructed a mock wall on the Diag designed to represent the barrier that separates Israel from Gaza and the West bank. Organizers said the wall was meant to bring attention to the history of Israeli violence against Palestinians.

The event included speeches from students, personal stories and information about the ongoing Israeli military campaign in Gaza. The event also featured a teach-in on the wall itself, which borders the West Bank for more than 700 kilometers and contains Israeli-run checkpoints. Israel has used these checkpoints and other obstacles to levy additional travel restrictions on Palestinians since Hamas’s Oct. 7 attack. The event’s mock wall, which featured artwork and statistics about Palestinian deaths, land loss and food insecurity, displayed messages like “We will return” painted across the barrier.

LSA senior Rawan Elhusseini, director of operations for SAFE, told The Michigan Daily in an interview that the demonstration aimed to bring attention to the broader context of violence against Palestinians that has persisted decades prior to the recent escalations. 

“This wall is a representation of the apartheid wall that’s up in the West Bank, and we hold this event every year,” Elhusseini said. “We’ve been holding this for years, talking and showing people the atrocities happening in the West Bank. This isn’t something new to us. It didn’t start on Oct. 7.”

Rackham student Ira Anjali Anwar, a member of the TAHRIR Coalition — a group of more than 90 pro-Palestine organizations — and the co-chair of the Graduate Employees’ Organization’s Solidarity and Political Action Committee, explained the use of the word apartheid when describing the border wall in an interview with The Daily.

“It’s primarily an event for political education to bring awareness to the particular nature of the apartheid that has been going on in Palestine for over 75 years under Israeli occupation,” Anwar said. “While there are parallels with apartheid (in) South Africa, for instance, the way apartheid has functioned in Palestine is unique, because it’s not so much just about separating Israelis and Palestinians, but it’s about the fragmentation and isolation of Palestinians themselves.”

Several students stood alongside the wall holding informational cards. According to Anwar, this was an effort to both highlight details of the impact of Israeli occupation and to evoke the image of a human wall. 

“I think it’s beautiful because there’s an attempt to try and illustrate as visually as possible to depict what it would be like to be in apartheid Palestine and Israeli occupation,” Anwar said. 

In an interview with The Daily, LSA freshman Ayan Kamdar said she attended the event not only to support Palestine but also the people of Lebanon. In the last two weeks, Israeli forces have launched operations on the Hezbollah militant group throughout the country, killing more than 720 and wounding more than 1,835, according to Lebanon’s Health Ministry.

“When is this just gonna end?” Kamdar said. “It’s been a year almost, and now there’s new bombings in other countries like Lebanon and Syria. It’s just basic human nature that you don’t want people to get killed anymore.”

During the event, U-M alum Svetlana Toder, a parent of a current student and a former U-M employee, held an Israeli flag while standing next to the mock wall. In an interview with The Daily, Toder said she believed the event could make Jewish students feel unsafe, so she brought the flag as a show of support for them. 

“Unfortunately, events like the one happening right now — while it seems innocent and it seems like they’re exercising their freedom of speech — they actually incite a lot of hate and violence for Jewish students,” Toder said. “The reason why I came today is because a lot of Jewish students expressed concerns about walking throughout campus and being identified as Jewish, and I came here … to show Jewish students and students that support Israel that they have somebody on their side.”

Social Work student Helen Shanker said she attended the event to express her solidarity with the Palestinian cause. Shanker, a Jewish student, said she hoped the mock apartheid wall would draw attention to the ongoing violence in Gaza. 

“As a Jewish member of the community that is advocating for Palestine, I think it’s really important to show up, especially a year since Oct. 7, and many years into the genocide,” Shanker said. “I want to honor the stories and the art that represents the very real apartheid wall surrounding the West Bank. It occupies and imprisons many human beings without rights or access to ample food and water.”

column3: Attacks on Lebanon spark protest at Angell Hall
column7:
The University Insider is The Daily’s first faculty and staff-oriented newsletter. This weekly newsletter will give U-M faculty and staff the ability to see the most important issues on campus and in Ann Arbor — particularly those related to administrative decisions — from the perspective of an independent news organization. It will also provide a better understanding of student perspectives.








Editor’s Note: Maryam Shafie is a former Daily staffer. Shafie did not contribute to this article.

Nearly 100 protesters gathered at the steps of Angell Hall on Sept. 23 as part of a national call to action from leaders of the Palestinian Youth Movement following attacks on Lebanon last week. The protest was organized by Students Allied for Freedom and Equality and the TAHRIR Coalition, composed of more than 90 pro-Palestine groups. 

Israel carried out a coordinated attack in Lebanon Sept. 17 during which the pagers of thousands of Hezbollah members exploded. The explosions killed a total of 12 people and wounded 2,800. The Sept. 17 attack was followed by another attack the next day in which walkie-talkies were detonated, killing at least 20 people and wounding more than 450. The explosive materials were placed inside the communication devices prior to their arrival in Lebanon, according to officials briefed on the situation. 

Hezbollah and Israel have been trading fire since Hamas’ Oct. 7 attacks on Israel, but the events of this past week mark a significant escalation. Sept. 23 marked one of the deadliest days in Lebanon since the Israel-Hezbollah conflict in 2006. At least 558 people were killed on Monday, and, as of Sept. 28, the overall death toll has risen to 720, according to Lebanon’s Health Ministry. 

Since Sept. 17, more than 1,800 people have been wounded in Israel’s attacks on Lebanon. Moreover, since Oct. 7, 2023, approximately 500,000 people have been displaced from Lebanon, according to Lebanese authorities. 

On Friday, Sept. 27, Israel launched an air-strike on Hezbollah headquarters in Beirut, killing Hezbollah leader Hassan Nasrallah. Hezbollah said they will continue to attack Israel as long as they continue their ongoing military campaign in Gaza.

Engineering senior Maryam Shafie began the event by speaking to protesters about the connection between Lebanon and Palestine. Shafie reminded protesters that Israel attacked Lebanon in 2006 and attributed the recent assault on Lebanon to Israeli attempts to destabilize the region.  

“Today, Lebanon is bleeding because of its unwavering stance with the people of Palestine,” Shafie said. “The pain we witness mirrors the horrors that have plagued our Palestinian brothers and sisters for the past year. The same cruel genocidal tactics, the same fabricated stories used to justify the genocide in Gaza are now being spun to defend the crimes in Lebanon.”

Shafie emphasized that the fights against violence occurring in Lebanon and Palestine are connected, and applauded the resilience of civilians in those areas. 

“Lebanon and Gaza are united not just in their suffering but in their strength, their shared struggle for a future where their people are free, where their land is promised to them and where their children can wave to the sounds of birds instead of missiles,” Shafie said. “This fight is more than a struggle for survival, it is a testament to the courage of a people refusing to be silenced.” 

The start of Shafie’s speech was delayed as event organizers and the University of Michigan’s Division of Public Safety and Security were engaged in a conflict over usage of noise amplification devices to deliver speeches. 

In an email to The Michigan Daily, University spokesperson Colleen Mastony said the University welcomes dissent, advocacy and the expression of ideas, including those that are unpopular, upsetting or critical of the University. Jarvis made clear that although the University supports freedom of speech, students are held accountable for nonacademic misconduct under the Statement of Student Right and Responsibilities. 

“On Sept. 24, the Division of Public Safety and Security advised the group on the steps of Angell Hall that if they used amplified sound they could be given a verbal warning for trespass for disrupting university operations,” Mastony wrote. “Classes were being held at that time.” 

The protesters ultimately agreed to forego usage of megaphones and other voice amplifiers. 

In an interview with The Daily, a SAFE media representative shared concerns about the presence of law enforcement officers at the protest. The source requested to remain anonymous citing fear of retaliation and potential for heightened scrutiny and surveillance. 

“The protest remained peaceful throughout, yet the police presence was high,” the representative said. “It sends a concerning message when law enforcement responds so heavily to students exercising their right to protest, especially when the focus of the demonstration is often related to issues of policing and systemic injustice.”

Over the past year SAFE, the TAHRIR Coalition and other pro-Palestine activist organizations have clashed with local law enforcement. Last month, one U-M community member and three other individuals who were not affiliated with the University were arrested during a pro-Palestine “die-in” at Festifall. In May, DPSS and the Ann Arbor Police Department used pepper spray on protesters while clearing the Gaza solidarity encampment that had been set up on the Diag. Protesters were also arrested at a demonstration outside the University of Michigan Art Museum in May and a sit-in at Ruthven in November. The SAFE media representative emphasized that the presence of law enforcement will not prevent the organization from expressing their views. 

“This attempt at intimidation aims to discourage participation and voices that most need to be heard, but we refuse to be silenced,” the representative said. “Ultimately, it’s crucial to reiterate that today’s protest was in solidarity with the people of Lebanon and Palestine, uplifting their resistance under the current Zionist oppression and honoring the martyrs in the struggle for liberation.”

Although the speeches given during Tuesday’s protest focused on the political context of the assault on Lebanon, some speakers shared more personal connections. LSA senior Rawan El-Husseini spoke about her family in Lebanon. 

“At this current moment my grandmother remains in southern Lebanon,” El-Husseini said. “My grandpa and aunt, before they fled, urged her to leave. I learned a lot from my grandma growing up, but yesterday, I learned the most important lesson yet. Staying and enduring the bombing, even potentially losing her life was worth more to her than fleeing and leaving her house and her land. Today, as I stand here, I hold my grandma’s spirit in my heart, she is a reminder that Lebanese people are born with resistance in their blood … just like Palestinians.” 

Following the speeches, protesters marched on South University Avenue and through the Diag, ending the rally at the steps of Angell Hall.

Attacks on Lebanon spark protest at Angell Hall
The University Insider is The Daily’s first faculty and staff-oriented newsletter. This weekly newsletter will give U-M faculty and staff the ability to see the most important issues on campus and in Ann Arbor — particularly those related to administrative decisions — from the perspective of an independent news organization. It will also provide a better understanding of student perspectives.








Editor’s Note: <PERSON> is a former Daily staffer. Shafie did not contribute to this article.

Nearly 100 protesters gathered at the steps of Angell Hall on Sept. 23 as part of a national call to action from leaders of the Palestinian Youth Movement following attacks on Lebanon last week. The protest was organized by Students Allied for Freedom and Equality and the TAHRIR Coalition, composed of more than 90 pro-Palestine groups. 

Israel carried out a coordinated attack in Lebanon Sept. 17 during which the pagers of thousands of Hezbollah members exploded. The explosions killed a total of 12 people and wounded 2,800. The Sept. 17 attack was followed by another attack the next day in which walkie-talkies were detonated, killing at least 20 people and wounding more than 450. The explosive materials were placed inside the communication devices prior to their arrival in Lebanon, according to officials briefed on the situation. 

Hezbollah and Israel have been trading fire since Hamas’ Oct. 7 attacks on Israel, but the events of this past week mark a significant escalation. Sept. 23 marked one of the deadliest days in Lebanon since the Israel-Hezbollah conflict in 2006. At least 558 people were killed on Monday, and, as of Sept. 28, the overall death toll has risen to 720, according to Lebanon’s Health Ministry. 

Since Sept. 17, more than 1,800 people have been wounded in Israel’s attacks on Lebanon. Moreover, since Oct. 7, 2023, approximately 500,000 people have been displaced from Lebanon, according to Lebanese authorities. 

On Friday, Sept. 27, Israel launched an air-strike on Hezbollah headquarters in Beirut, killing Hezbollah leader <PERSON>. Hezbollah said they will continue to attack Israel as long as they continue their ongoing military campaign in Gaza.

Engineering senior <PERSON> began the event by speaking to protesters about the connection between Lebanon and Palestine. <PERSON> reminded protesters that Israel attacked Lebanon in 2006 and attributed the recent assault on Lebanon to Israeli attempts to destabilize the region.  

“Today, Lebanon is bleeding because of its unwavering stance with the people of Palestine,” <PERSON> said. “The pain we witness mirrors the horrors that have plagued our Palestinian brothers and sisters for the past year. The same cruel genocidal tactics, the same fabricated stories used to justify the genocide in Gaza are now being spun to defend the crimes in Lebanon.”

<PERSON> emphasized that the fights against violence occurring in Lebanon and Palestine are connected, and applauded the resilience of civilians in those areas. 

“Lebanon and Gaza are united not just in their suffering but in their strength, their shared struggle for a future where their people are free, where their land is promised to them and where their children can wave to the sounds of birds instead of missiles,” Shafie said. “This fight is more than a struggle for survival, it is a testament to the courage of a people refusing to be silenced.” 

The start of <PERSON>’s speech was delayed as event organizers and the University of Michigan’s Division of Public Safety and Security were engaged in a conflict over usage of noise amplification devices to deliver speeches. 

In an email to The Michigan Daily, University spokesperson <PERSON> said the University welcomes dissent, advocacy and the expression of ideas, including those that are unpopular, upsetting or critical of the University. <PERSON> made clear that although the University supports freedom of speech, students are held accountable for nonacademic misconduct under the Statement of Student Right and Responsibilities. 

“On Sept. 24, the Division of Public Safety and Security advised the group on the steps of Angell Hall that if they used amplified sound they could be given a verbal warning for trespass for disrupting university operations,” Mastony wrote. “Classes were being held at that time.” 

The protesters ultimately agreed to forego usage of megaphones and other voice amplifiers. 

In an interview with The Daily, a SAFE media representative shared concerns about the presence of law enforcement officers at the protest. The source requested to remain anonymous citing fear of retaliation and potential for heightened scrutiny and surveillance. 

“The protest remained peaceful throughout, yet the police presence was high,” the representative said. “It sends a concerning message when law enforcement responds so heavily to students exercising their right to protest, especially when the focus of the demonstration is often related to issues of policing and systemic injustice.”

“This attempt at intimidation aims to discourage participation and voices that most need to be heard, but we refuse to be silenced,” the representative said. “Ultimately, it’s crucial to reiterate that today’s protest was in solidarity with the people of Lebanon and Palestine, uplifting their resistance under the current Zionist oppression and honoring the martyrs in the struggle for liberation.”

“At this current moment my grandmother remains in southern Lebanon,” El-Husseini said. “My grandpa and aunt, before they fled, urged her to leave. I learned a lot from my grandma growing up, but yesterday, I learned the most important lesson yet. Staying and enduring the bombing, even potentially losing her life was worth more to her than fleeing and leaving her house and her land. Today, as I stand here, I hold my grandma’s spirit in my heart, she is a reminder that Lebanese people are born with resistance in their blood … just like Palestinians.”

column3: Jewish Resource Center hosts ‘Together We Rise’ on Diag
column7:
Content warning: This article includes mentions of violence.

More than 500 University of Michigan community members gathered on the Diag Friday evening for a rally celebrating Jewish unity in the face of recent antisemitism on campus. The rally, hosted by the Jewish Resource Center, was planned for parents and family weekend to provide an opportunity for families to celebrate their community and heritage together, according to Rabbi Yitz Pierce. “These events are critical for students to see that we have strength,” Pierce said. “As a minority group on campus, if you just go hide in the shadows, then you just feel that much more pushed down or oppressed. If we come together as a large group and show our strength, I think everyone is lifted up through that.”

LSA junior Daniela Portnoy, JRC president, said in an interview with The Daily that she hoped the rally would bring the Jewish community on campus together following recent assaults on Jewish students in Ann Arbor.

“Our entire purpose of this event was to come together as a community and show parents — especially — that are here this weekend, that we are strong and we are proud to be Jewish, and this is a safe place for our students to be,” Portnoy said. “And while this was a very rough week — we had multiple assaults on Jewish students — and rising antisemitism within the country and issues in Israel, we thought that this was the way to do it. And together we will rise to overcome everything.”

In her remarks at the event, Portnoy expressed feeling a sense of fear as a Jewish student after Hamas’ Oct. 7 attack on Israel. Portnoy said she found comfort and joy through the Jewish community at the University.

“Jewish joy is resilience,” Portnoy said. “It’s the joy of overflowing and overcrowded Shabbat dinners. … It’s the joy of feeling connected to people across the world, it’s the joy of tradition, culture and community that has sustained our people through countless challenges. Jewish joy is an act of defiance. It’s the ability to celebrate even when the world feels heavy, to say that we are here, we will thrive, and we will hold onto the traditions that make us proud to be Jewish.”

LSA sophomore Gabe Silverstein, president of Sigma Alpha Mu, said he feels a strong connection among the Jewish community on campus, whose traditions remain vibrant during difficult times. 

“What they don’t see, and what has become increasingly apparent over the last year, is not the existence of Jewish hate, but the persistence of Jewish celebration,” Silverstein said. “The unity among Jews on this campus is what keeps us all afloat amidst an existential flood of negativity, hate and darkness.”

Rabbi Fully Eisenberger said in addition to more displays of Jewish pride on campus over the last year, Jewish people continue to daven, or pray, and take action to bring the hostages taken by Hamas on Oct. 7 home. As of last week, at least 97 of the 251 people taken hostage are still being held in Gaza, at least 33 of whom are presumed dead. 

“Many people don’t even know that every single day, Jews are davening, they’re praying, to make these soldiers safe and to bring the hostages home,” Eisenberger said. “That’s what we do. We’re one nation. We care about each other.”

Maya and Itay Regev, two siblings who were taken hostage by Hamas at the Nova Music Festival in Israel on Oct. 7, 2023, told the crowd about their experiences. As their father stood with them, Maya said she and her brother were separated when they were taken to Gaza and held hostage for more than 50 days while suffering from gunshot wounds.

“I was alone, and the only thing that kept me going was thinking about my family, thinking about the people in Israel, that I know that they are doing something to help me,” Regev said. “I know we have an amazing community, an amazing country, and they will do anything to help me and all the other hostages.”

Jen Airley, the mother of an Israeli soldier who died in combat, encouraged other members of the Jewish community to perform acts of kindness and mitzvot, the 613 commandments in the Torah. 

“I’m asking you to take on one thing, one commitment,” Airley said. “I want you to take on something, whether it’s a mitzvah, whether it’s an act of kindness … because that is going to be your weapon in fighting this war.”

column3: University President Santa Ono responds to Instagram deleting JVP post
column7:
The University Insider is The Daily’s first faculty and staff-oriented newsletter. This weekly newsletter will give U-M faculty and staff the ability to see the most important issues on campus and in Ann Arbor — particularly those related to administrative decisions — from the perspective of an independent news organization. It will also provide a better understanding of student perspectives.








University of Michigan President Santa Ono responded to a recently deleted Instagram post by the Jewish Voice for Peace at the University of Michigan in a statement released Sunday.

On its Instagram story, JVP reposted an Instagram post calling for “death to Israel.” Instagram removed the post. According to Instagram’s Community Guidelines, the company reserves the right to remove posts that contain credible threats or hate speech.

Ono wrote in the statement that the University supports Instagram’s decision to remove the post. 

“We find the post repugnant and support Instagram’s decision to delete the post,” Ono wrote.

Ono also confirmed that JVP has not been officially registered as a U-M student organization since 2016 and has now been removed from Maize Pages to reflect this change.

JVP and the TAHRIR Coalition, a group of more than 90 pro-Palestine organizations, did not respond to request for comment in time for publication.

column3: TAHRIR Coalition hosts vigil to honor lost Palestinian lives
column7:
About 50 University of Michigan students and community members gathered on the steps of Angell Hall Thursday evening for a vigil in honor of the thousands of Palestinians who have died during the 11-month-long Israeli military campaign in Gaza. The vigil was organized by the TAHRIR Coalition, a coalition of more than 90 pro-Palestine organizations.

The vigil comes after Gaza’s health ministry released a 649-page document listing names of and other identifying information about 34,344 Palestinians killed in Gaza since Oct. 7. 

At about 9:20 p.m., several speakers addressed the crowd from the top of the Angell Hall stairs.  Attendees held Palestinian flags and posters with photos of victims from Gaza while gathering around an outline of historical Palestine at the base of the steps. 

Engineering senior Maryam Shafie spoke to attendees about the ongoing toll of the conflict, emphasizing the grief experienced by those in Gaza. 

“We must remind ourselves that death is not a rare or isolated occurrence in Palestine,” Shafie said. “It is a relentless, ongoing reality in Gaza. Grief is immeasurable, eternal. It is a constant, unimaginable reality woven into the fabric of daily life. … The people of Gaza are not forgotten. Their struggle is our struggle. Their pain is our pain. We cannot and will not turn away. Hope still lives in Gaza, and as long as it does, it must live within us, too.”

Ann Arbor resident Eirha Wright told The Daily she wanted to attend Thursday’s vigil to commemorate the thousands of Palestinians killed by the Israeli military.

“We just wanted to come here to honor the victims of the genocide and pay the respects that they so much deserve,” Wright said. “I’m interested in maybe a moment of silence and potential reading of names.”

In an interview with The Michigan Daily, Vincent Salgado, a research associate at the Graham Sustainability Institute who attended the vigil, reflected on his Chicano and Mexican background and emphasized the importance of solidarity among marginalized communities. 

“For me, I’m from a Chicano and Mexican background, and I’d like to show our Palestinian community members that people from my background have their back,” Salgado said. “In Spanish, we have a saying that, ‘one who does not know love cannot understand martyrism.’ There’s many people, unfortunately, throughout Michigan and the U.S., who cannot understand the struggle of Palestinians because they have not learned to love those who are not like themselves.”

After the speeches, speakers shared original poems recounting personal experiences with the Israeli attacks in Gaza, followed by prayer to commemorate the lives lost.

In her speech, Shafie said it was important to maintain awareness and empathy for the suffering of Palestinians, urging people not to grow numb or indifferent to loss and grief.

“Every single martyr — a soul ripped from the world too soon,” Shafie said. “This cannot become normal to us. We cannot allow ourselves to grow numb, to let the shock fade or to become immune to the suffering of Palestinians. Our movement must remain rooted in the humanity of those for whom we fight. This is a movement of grief, a grief that transcends the borders of Gaza, one that links us to every one of our martyrs.”

Correction 9/23: This article has been updated to reflect that attendees gathered around an outline of historical Palestine.
