column3: ‘We will still be here’: More than one hundred protesters picket outside Regent’s meeting
column7:
The University Insider is The Daily’s first faculty and staff-oriented newsletter. This weekly newsletter will give U-M faculty and staff the ability to see the most important issues on campus and in Ann Arbor — particularly those related to administrative decisions — from the perspective of an independent news organization. It will also provide a better understanding of student perspectives.








At the back entrance of the Alexander G. Ruthven Building, about seven feet separated 13 state police officers from more than 100 student protesters, Palestinian flags and chants. 

More than 100 University of Michigan students and community members continued months of protests against the University for its decision not to divest from companies profiting from the Israeli military campaign in Gaza, gathering in a picketing circle before the Board of Regents’ meeting outside Ruthven Thursday morning. Barriers covered in multiple flyers displaying QR codes linked to a Statement of Student Rights and Responsibilities, the Standard Practice Guide and the Michigan State Trespass Law held back protesters as their chants rang throughout North University Avenue.

After the meeting began, students marched to the back of the building, where they chanted “If we don’t get it, shut it down” and “Divestment is our demand, no peace on stolen land” for two hours. The protest was hosted by the TAHRIR Coalition, a coalition of more than 90 pro-Palestine organizations, including Jewish Voice for Peace and Students Allied for Freedom and Equality.

The board held its March 28 meeting at 9 a.m., seven hours earlier than its typical 4 p.m. start time. University spokesperson Kim Broekhuizen wrote in an email to The Michigan Daily that the meeting time was changed due to scheduling conflicts.

In an interview with The Daily at the protest, SAFE president Salma Hamamy said she believes the University administration changed the meeting time to guard against protesters, who have demonstrated at the past few Regents meetings.

“They did this to explicitly miss interfering with their students, especially students who are extremely angry with their recent protocols and the way that they have overall been treating their students on campus,” Hamamy said.

Hamamy said students remained committed to protesting despite the time change and early start to the meeting. 

“Students came here to let them know that it doesn’t matter if you change the meeting to 9 a.m., 10 a.m., whatever time it might be,” Hamamy said. “We will still be here, and we will still show up bright and early in the morning.” 

Hamamy spoke about the atmosphere outside Ruthven, including the barriers and QR codes displayed on them.

“The police up at the front have basically formed a barricade to prevent protesters from moving any further,” Hamamy said. “Thankfully, there has been no escalation so far, but knowing how the police have operated in the past could easily flip at any moment.”

Hamamy told The Daily she expects little from the Regents because she believes they will continue to prioritize University finances over student voices. 

“I expect them to remain as disappointing as ever,” Hamamy said. “They have shown time and time again that they do not care about human life and continue to prioritize profit over anything else. So long as they do that, they can expect us to come out here, but we know that they’re not going to instill any change or implement any difference or listen to any of our students. Because that is not what they actually are here for. They’re here to make money. It’s very unfortunate leaving their students outside constantly trying to demand a veto.”

According to SAFE board members at Thursday morning’s protest, three students were given citations from police for trespassing at the 101st Honor Convocation when a protest disrupted the event at Hill Auditorium Sunday. 

In an interview with The Daily, one student said they were only aware they could not enter Ruthven after the Nov. 17 protest inside the building, but the ticket they received on Thursday morning stated that they were banned from five campus buildings. The student has requested anonymity for fear of retribution.

“I never received a warning,” the student said. “I was blindsided by this ticket. … While I was walking with my friend, a police officer grabbed me and informed me that I was banned from five university buildings.”

In an email to The Daily, Melissa Overton, Division of Public Safety and Security deputy police chief, declined to provide additional information on the citations, citing an ongoing investigation.

column3: UMich Board of Regents discusses divestment, departing officers
column7:
The University Insider is The Daily’s first faculty and staff-oriented newsletter. This weekly newsletter will give U-M faculty and staff the ability to see the most important issues on campus and in Ann Arbor — particularly those related to administrative decisions — from the perspective of an independent news organization. It will also provide a better understanding of student perspectives.








The University of Michigan Board of Regents met Thursday morning in the Alexander G. Ruthven building to discuss their continued decision to not divest from companies with financial ties to Israel, resolutions honoring departing executive officers and schematics for the new University of Michigan Center for Innovation.

In response to continued student protests, University Regent Sarah Hubbard (R) reaffirmed that the Finance, Audit and Investment Committee has decided not to divest from companies profiting off Israel’s military campaign in Gaza. In her remarks announcing the decision, Hubbard said the main focus of the endowment is on profitability.

“We do not make this decision lightly,” Hubbard said. “Together we must work to address the most complex challenges of our time. We do this through active engagement with one another and with researchers in countries around the world. After deliberation, we have decided to stand by our long standing policy. We will continue to shield the endowment from political pressures and base our investment decisions on financial factors such as risk and return. We are not moving to make any divestment of any kind from the University endowment.”

In comments following the announcement, University Regent Michael Behm (D) responded to statements from the U-M community regarding Israel-related investments. One of the statements Behm responded to included the figure that $6 billion of the endowment is invested in what a Jewish Voice for Peace Instagram post calls “the Israeli regime.” Behm said the $6 billion figure refers to investments in companies that do business with Israel, not Israeli companies themselves.

“We have no direct investment in any Israeli companies,” Behm said. “What we do have are funds (that Israeli companies) may be a part of. … I asked the endowment managers and in reality, less than one-tenth of 1% is in such companies.”

University Regent Denise Illitch (D) then spoke in support of the creation of a varsity women’s hockey team at the University. The prospect is currently undergoing a feasibility study. University President Santa Ono and members of the board, such as Regent Jordan Acker (D), said they supported the study during Thursday’s meeting. 

“The time has come and this is part of our service to the people to have a women’s hockey team and not force our elite female hockey players to go elsewhere,” Acker said.

The Board of Regents also passed two resolutions honoring two departing executive officers. The first is Sally Churchill, vice president and secretary, who is retiring after serving as vice president and secretary since May 2005. The other resolution honors Dr. Rebecca Cunningham, vice president for research and innovation, who has recently accepted a position as university president at the University of Minnesota. The resolutions were followed by remarks from the board thanking them for their time at the University. In her remarks about Churchill, Illitch thanked Churchill for her dedication to the University and her management of the eight U-M Regents.

“You have been able to manage eight very independent thinkers and strong-minded leaders as well as the president and officer team,” Illitch said. “It’s a lot of balance, and you’ve done just an exemplary job.”

The design for the UMCI was also approved by the board. The UMCI, which will house graduate programs, offices and shared spaces for community engagement and public projects, broke ground in December and is estimated to be completed in 2027. University Provost Laurie McCauley said the UMCI will expand on academic and career opportunities in Detroit.

“The UMCI will bring together the best of the University of Michigan to catalyze economic development in the city of Detroit,” McCauley said. “It will house a robust portfolio of academic programs, including new interdisciplinary graduate degrees and certificate programs aligned to the skills of the future and needed to attract startups and major employers to the region.”

Meera Herle, Central Student Government president, presented the final report of her term and highlighted her administration’s past accomplishments. Herle expressed disappointment with University’s policies around the Israel-Hamas war, including a draft policy that would prohibit any disruptions of University operations like the TAHRIR Coalition’s protest during the University’s 101st Honors Convocation.

“Recently, it feels as though the board has been making decisions on behalf of our campus from an emotionally charged place,” Herle said. “By being reactive decision makers, you are encouraging students to reciprocate this behavior, and you are escalating tensions. While as members of the board, you represent constituents external to this university, you also have an obligation to make sure that you are taking care of our campus community.”

The Statement of Student Rights and Responsibilities is usually amended every three years, but can be amended during an off-cycle year if the vice president of Student Life, chair of the Student Relations Advisory Committee on the Faculty Senate and the CSG president agree on a new amendment period. According to Herle, the administration has not contacted her about amending the policy, despite the fact that their Disruptive Action Policy would alter student rights on campus. In her closing remarks, Herle said she felt disappointed with the lack of communication between the board and students and was concerned that external opinions are valued over student input.

“I’m ending my term frustrated with leadership at (the University) for not treating students of different perspectives equally and not valuing student input above the opinions of people external to our university,” Herle said. “I intend on working with my successor to ensure that knowledge about the dynamic between the board and Student Government is something that they are familiar with and which they are equipped to handle.”

Herle said she hopes to see the board’s relationship with students improve in the future.

“I meet with leaders every day who truly care about the student experience,” Herle said. “It makes me hopeful that one day the most senior ranks of leadership at (the University) will better understand how to serve those who need their support the most.”

column3: Santa Ono talks Honors Convocation, student housing shortages in exclusive interview with The Michigan Daily
column7:
The University Insider is The Daily’s first faculty and staff-oriented newsletter. This weekly newsletter will give U-M faculty and staff the ability to see the most important issues on campus and in Ann Arbor — particularly those related to administrative decisions — from the perspective of an independent news organization. It will also provide a better understanding of student perspectives.








The Michigan Daily sat down with University President Santa Ono Monday morning to discuss the interruption by student protesters at the 101st Honors Convocation, student housing shortages, basketball coach Dusty May and Ono’s proudest accomplishments and his shortcomings this past semester. The Daily provided Ono with the questions prior to the interview. This interview has been edited and condensed for clarity.

The Michigan Daily: On Feb. 15, the TAHRIR Coalition launched a Divest, Don’t Arrest campaign to ask the University of Michigan administration to divest from the Israeli military. Other initiatives and campaigns, like the Faculty Senate vote, student protests and other petitions, have asked for the same. What impact does this have on the decision of whether or not to divest?

Santa Ono: The decision about divestment is something that’s in the purview of the Board of Regents, and certainly, if they’re not made aware of these decisions or the statements or resolutions directly, I certainly do make them aware of all of them. I think that the Board of Regents listens to those concerns and they factor all those into their decision. At this time, they have decided not to change course with their investment decisions. So to answer your question, yes, they are heard, they’re shared, and the Board of Regents reviews them in their decision making process.

TMD: On March 14, statements from MHousing were sent to certain residence halls on campus addressing an unauthorized flier distribution. The flier encouraged students, faculty and staff to register to vote in the People’s Referendum, which opened on March 20 and will close on March 28. What was your reaction to this message? How do you think this will affect the campus community?

SO: I haven’t actually seen the flier myself, but I understand from Martino Harmon, the vice president for Student Life, that there was a communication that was sent out to housing staff following that regarding expectations in terms of distribution of such flyers, and so I appreciate his leadership in managing the situation.

TMD: About 100 protestors interrupted the 101st Honors Convocation on Sunday afternoon. How did this make you feel? 

SO: This is an institution where people voice their opinions, and I understand that the current geopolitical situation in the Middle East is very difficult. That’s been evident in the many demonstrations and protests throughout the academic year, which I respect. It’s very sad that it happened at the Honors Convocation because I know many of the students personally, and I know many of their parents. It was something they were looking forward to, and their parents were looking forward to celebrating their children’s achievements. It was clear from watching their reactions that they were saddened by it. Some were shaken. I want students to celebrate their achievements, and I want parents to celebrate the achievements of their kids.

TMD: Will there be further preventative measures at future events?

SO: I think that’s something that’s under active discussion right now. Certainly, there have been a number of students, friends and relatives who have asked for us to review what happened at the Honors Convocation. There are other events coming up that are just as, or maybe even more, impactful and meaningful to graduating students. I think it’s appropriate for them to ask us to look at what happened and for us to review that, and certainly we will communicate that at the appropriate time.

TMD: On Feb. 21, the University announced that it was formally switching to a test-optional policy. How do you think this change will impact the admissions process going forward?

SO: A number of other universities have already announced, or are in the process of making, their decision regarding test optional policies. Our decision to remain test-optional is based upon a rigorous evaluation of our own data. We’ve actually looked at that data and for our institution it doesn’t really make sense to make tests mandatory. At other schools they have data which shows otherwise, and based upon that, they decided that requiring tests helps them to make the admissions decision to determine the likelihood that the students will be retained and succeed and thrive at their institutions. The data is very robust, and so I support the decision made by the Office of Admissions at Michigan. Obviously one very important driver for us is that the test optional status has been very important to recruiting a great diverse group of students. 

TMD: Rebecca Cunningham, former U-M Vice President for Research and Innovation, left the University to become the president of the University of Minnesota. What qualities are you looking for in a new vice president for research and innovation, and what is your plan to find someone to fill the role?

SO: I’m just so happy for Rebecca for being named the president of the University of Minnesota, another Big 10 institution, which is an excellent university. It’s a reflection of how she and the University of Michigan are viewed by one of the peer institutions. I think her over two decades of service at the University are exemplary, and she’s a great researcher. I really enjoyed having her as part of my team here over the past year and a half. 

One of the things I’m looking at is a balance of expertise in the leadership team. I’m a medical scientist. Rebecca is a medical doctor. U-M Provost Laurie McCauley is a dentist. Lot of medical scientists, so we’re natural science heavy. One of the things I’m weighing in addition to leadership skills, vision, innovation and creativity is the balance of disciplinary expertise among my leadership team. Right now, I’m evaluating possibilities for internal leadership, and interviewing some people. Hopefully I can make an interim appointment soon because Rebecca’s transition is at the end of the month, just around the corner. Then there’ll be a period where I’ll evaluate that individual’s performance, perhaps consider other people in a broader search and then make a decision about permanent employment as vice president.

TMD: A University Record article stated that some U-M faculty have become the focus of pressure and attacks over their research and academic work. How does the University administration plan to support these faculty members? How do you plan to ensure that faculty members feel like they are free to express their opinions without facing retribution?

SO: Certainly something that faculty experience in universities around the world are geopolitical tensions that we have discussed, which have resulted in tensions not just within the student body, but also among faculty and staff. There’s also an increasing spotlight on people’s views, especially prominent faculty members and students. Faculty members have been doxxed, they’ve been harassed on social media, and certainly that’s something which we want to ensure doesn’t happen to the best of our ability. For that reason, the provost has actually, prior to that Senate resolution on academic freedom, released information about our commitment to protecting faculty and supporting them during these difficult times. I support the provost’s efforts. It troubles me whenever a faculty member or student experiences these sorts of attacks, or microaggressions, and I think supporting faculty and addressing these attacks is the right thing for our university to do.

TMD: This past year the University’s enrollment numbers topped 52,000 students, making it the largest school in the state of Michigan. With the incoming class of 2027, some expect numbers to rise. How is the University working to accommodate the growing student population?

SO: I used to be in charge of enrollment management and admissions at Emory University in Atlanta. Anybody that’s in that space knows it’s an inexact science. It’s kind of an art because you really don’t know whether someone you offer admission to is going to come or not. The deans set targets for the numbers of students on an annual basis, and they come up with a number. That’s the way it should be; it shouldn’t come from me, it should really come from what can be supported within a department or program and also take into account our capability to support them. I can tell you that we do not want to grow the ultimate number of students this year, for a variety of reasons. We over-yielded last year. We have more students than we had anticipated. To try to address some of the pressures in terms of student housing affordability, making sure that we have enough faculty and support services, we’re trying to keep enrollment flat. We’re trying to not grow to ensure that we can support students that are here, and the ones that come in, but the ultimate number who end up here really depends upon yield and melt.

TMD: How do you think that this largest class size has affected campus?

SO: There hasn’t been a lot of growth in terms of first- and second-year housing, or to the residence halls for the longest time. Many of our residence halls are relatively old. I wish that what we’re doing now — with one of the largest residence hall construction projects in the country, with thousands of new rooms and in phase two, which will have a thousand more rooms — had started a little bit earlier. But it takes time for us to actually finish building. We realize that there are also some pressures in terms of availability of off-campus housing and the cost of that. That’s why we’re trying to address it by aggressively growing the number of rooms that we can offer to our students, and like I said, I wish we had started earlier. I should say that the schools and the student welfare staff are really good at knowing the number of students. They can adjust the number of instructional staff and they can try to adjust the amount of people who are there for advising, student support and otherwise. Even though it’s hard to know exactly how many students will be here, they also have the ability to adjust to the size of students that ultimately results.

TMD: Students have reported more difficulties with finding off-campus housing, and with higher demand for campus housing, many have run into a housing shortage. Is the University administration working to provide more housing for students? 

SO: We’re about to release two things: one is Campus Vision 2034 and the Campus Plan 2050, which is a multi-decade plan. Once it’s released, you’ll see that we have a long-term strategy for addressing housing in residence halls, but also to address housing for staff, which may relieve the pressure of off-campus housing and increase the availability for students. I think we will be releasing the campus vision first, and then staggered after that will be the campus plan, so it’s an important issue that we’re thinking about.

TMD: In your last interview with The Daily, you said the University administration sent a letter to Nike on Dec 8. Do you have any update about this?

SO: We’re awaiting their response, and we have made further contact. We remain focused on that. It’s important not only to the student group United Students Against Sweatshops but also to the committee that reports to me.

TMD: Why hasn’t the University cut ties with Nike?

SO: Waiting was the advice that came from the committee. They were wanting to see a little bit more data, and we haven’t received that yet, so we didn’t want to be premature. We wanted to give them a chance to respond. We have been a partner of theirs for some time, and so we’re awaiting their response and will continue to interact with them.

TMD: Were you involved in the decision to move on from Michigan men’s basketball coach Juwan Howard?

SO: I do have a responsibility as president to oversee athletics, and the athletic director, Warde Manuel and I, have a great relationship. We certainly were in conversation about the previous coach, coach performance and future during that period of time.

TMD: How do you feel about new basketball coach Dusty May?

SO: Similarly, Warde and I talked about potential prospects for the basketball coach following Juwan’s departure. I’m incredibly excited with Dusty’s selection. I love his energy. I love his integrity. I love what he’s been able to build at Florida Atlantic University. There’s a very good synergy between the kind of coach he is and what we were seeking. I think he’s going to get to work pretty quickly in building the team, and I’ve watched him and been very impressed with his coaching ability. I’m very optimistic about the future of basketball at the University of Michigan.

TMD: New Michigan football coach Sherrone Moore has brought in a lot of new faces to the coaching staff. What are your impressions of how the staff is rounded out?

SO: It’s remarkable. I mean, Sherrone Moore is a very young man, and I know him personally. I interviewed him before he was chosen and supported Warde in his selection of Sherrone. One of the questions people asked me when we were considering possible football coaches was about his relative youth, and I can tell you that he has addressed those concerns during this short period of time, and I think he has passed the test of managerial skills with flying colors. He was able to take a coaching staff that required a lot of renewal and to attract veteran talent in a very short period of time, which is the biggest test of any manager. 

TMD: As you approach the end of the winter 2024 semester at the University, what are your feelings about the past semester? 

SO: Time flies and I’m incredibly proud of so many different things. I’m proud of the leadership team that has been assembled. We have to recruit and appoint a new vice president of research, but I’m in the midst of considering who I might appoint. I’m really proud of the leadership team on it. I’m proud of how we’re doing in terms of research and teaching. We had multiple Rhodes Scholars and finalists and nominees. The quality of students is absolutely outstanding. I’m proud of some of the major initiatives like the near completion of the Campus Vision 2034 and Campus Plan. I can’t wait for people to see these when they come out in the next several weeks.

TMD: Where do you think you may have fallen short?

SO: I think handling some of the most challenging tasks that we’ve dealt with over the past 18 months, whether it has to do with athletics or the situation in the Middle East — it was a lot happening all at once. I wouldn’t say it’s fallen short, but if I were to look backwards, taking the time to calibrate the amount of time I was spending on each of these pretty heavy lifts is something that I think, if I were to do it again, I might have taken more time to think through everything. For me, there’s a lot of issues all at once. It’s a lot for the country to deal with, a lot for the student body. I’m no different from faculty and staff. We had to balance competing priorities for our time. If I was looking back I might have taken some more time to pause and think.

TMD: If you could take one class at the University, what would it be?

SO: That’s easy — an SMTD class with the orchestra.

<PERSON>: The decision about divestment is something that’s in the purview of the Board of Regents, and certainly, if they’re not made aware of these decisions or the statements or resolutions directly, I certainly do make them aware of all of them. I think that the Board of Regents listens to those concerns and they factor all those into their decision. At this time, they have decided not to change course with their investment decisions. So to answer your question, yes, they are heard, they’re shared, and the Board of Regents reviews them in their decision making process.

TMD: On March 14, statements from <PERSON> were sent to certain residence halls on campus addressing an unauthorized flier distribution. The flier encouraged students, faculty and staff to register to vote in the People’s Referendum, which opened on March 20 and will close on March 28. What was your reaction to this message? SO: I haven’t actually seen the flier myself, but I understand from <PERSON>, the vice president for Student Life, that there was a communication that was sent out to housing staff following that regarding expectations in terms of distribution of such flyers, and so I appreciate his leadership in managing the situation.

TMD: About 100 protestors interrupted the 101st Honors Convocation on Sunday afternoon. How did this make you feel? 

SO: This is an institution where people voice their opinions, and I understand that the current geopolitical situation in the Middle East is very difficult. That’s been evident in the many demonstrations and protests throughout the academic year, which I respect. It’s very sad that it happened at the Honors Convocation because I know many of the students personally, and I know many of their parents. It was something they were looking forward to, and their parents were looking forward to celebrating their children’s achievements. It was clear from watching their reactions that they were saddened by it. Some were shaken. I think it’s appropriate for them to ask us to look at what happened and for us to review that, and certainly we will communicate that at the appropriate time.

SO: A number of other universities have already announced, or are in the process of making, their decision regarding test optional policies. Our decision to remain test-optional is based upon a rigorous evaluation of our own data. We’ve actually looked at that data and for our institution it doesn’t really make sense to make tests mandatory. At other schools they have data which shows otherwise, and based upon that, they decided that requiring tests helps them to make the admissions decision to determine the likelihood that the students will be retained and succeed and thrive at their institutions. The data is very robust, and so I support the decision made by the Office of Admissions at Michigan. Obviously one very important driver for us is that the test optional status has been very important to recruiting a great diverse group of students. 

TMD: <PERSON>, former U-M Vice President for Research and Innovation, left the University to become the president of the University of Minnesota. What qualities are you looking for in a new vice president for research and innovation, and what is your plan to find someone to fill the role?

One of the things I’m looking at is a balance of expertise in the leadership team. I’m a medical scientist. <PERSON> is a medical doctor. U-M Provost <PERSON> is a dentist. One of the things I’m weighing in addition to leadership skills, vision, innovation and creativity is the balance of disciplinary expertise among my leadership team. Right now, I’m evaluating possibilities for internal leadership, and interviewing some people. column3: ‘In solidarity against the one employer’: About 100 gather for Union Solidarity Rally on Diag
column7:
The University Insider is The Daily’s first faculty and staff-oriented newsletter. This weekly newsletter will give U-M faculty and staff the ability to see the most important issues on campus and in Ann Arbor — particularly those related to administrative decisions — from the perspective of an independent news organization. It will also provide a better understanding of student perspectives.








100 labor union members and allies gathered on the Diag Saturday afternoon  in solidarity against their one common employer: the University of Michigan. U-M students, faculty and community members gathered to hear from union representatives to promote solidarity among unions against the University. The rally, which included the Lecturers’ Employees Organization, United Michigan Medicine Allied Professionals, Graduate Employees’ Organization, Service Employees International Union Healthcare Michigan and the U-M Professional Nurse Council, began on the Diag before marching down State Street.

The event was organized by the U-M chapter of Young Democratic Socialists Association and People’s Michigan. The rally kicked off with remarks from speakers representing each of the participating labor unions. In her opening speech, LEO president Kirsten Herold, lecturer for the School of Public Health, said lecturers have seen salary increases since first unionizing in 2004. 

“I want to tell you a little bit about what life was like for lecturers before our first contract in 2004,” Herold said. “Salaries of $14,000–$16,000 a year for a full teaching load. … There was really no job security. … We had no recourse if you got fired because you work contingent and temporary, and there was no support if you want to go to conferences or other things that cost money. Because of collective bargaining, we now have starting salaries in the low ($50,000s).”

LEO began its sixth bargaining cycle with the University in October. The union is expected to come to an agreement with the University for an updated contract by April 20. LEO signed their current contract in 2021, and ratified it in 2022. In their current negotiations, LEO is focused on higher wages, better working conditions and, most recently, pay parity among the three U-M campuses. 

In an interview with The Michigan Daily, international studies lecturer David Zeglen said he hopes the rally will bring attention to the pay disparity among workers doing similar jobs on different U-M campuses.

“I really want (the) administration to ensure that they don’t silo all the campuses the way that they’ve been doing for the budget model, saying that Flint should get this much money, and Dearborn should get this much money, and Arbor should get this much money,” Englin said. “It should be the same. (In) Dearborn and Flint, the lecturers there do more work than the lecturers at (U-M) Ann Arbor for full time load, they got paid significantly less when they have larger class sizes. That’s not fair.”

UMMAP President Penni Toney went to the rally to bring community awareness to how UMMAP exists not only to support workers, but to also look for the best practices in patient care. In an interview with The Daily during the rally, Tony said she wants the University administration to hold themselves to the same high standards that they expect from their employees.

“(The University) expects us to provide world class care, so (they) should treat us like the world class employees that we are,” Toney said. “Patient care is suffering due to the staffing crisis that is going on. We need more staff into the University and we need safer staffing ratios. … So that way, our patients are cared for better and our employees are at less risk.”

RAs are also in the process of collecting union authorization cards — documents that workers sign in support of a union to bargain on their behalf. If RAs receive at least 50% of signed cards, the group will unionize. In an interview with The Daily, LSA junior Mark Tallents, an RA in North Quad, said he came to the rally as part of a group of RAs who plan to vote to unionize.

“Our fight is one of the same: workers trying to organize for a more democratic institution,” Tallents said. “It’s our job to be here for them now that they need us and we’re sending a message to HR that they deserve a contract.”

LSA junior Nat Leach, president of U-M YDSA, and organizer of the event, spoke at the rally in support of the Divest! Don’t Arrest referendum. The tricampus referendum calls on the University administration to divest from companies with financial ties to Israel. In their speech, Leach called on the University to redirect these investments toward supporting workers.

“While we do not have all of labor power, we have people power and holding our own people’s referendum to call for divestment from these companies complacent (with) genocide, and instead demand that the University of Michigan invest that money into our lecturers, into our GSIs, into our hospital workers and into our own academic opportunities,” Leach said.

UMNPC does not plan to bargain until 2026, but union members went to the rally to support other unions. In an interview with The Daily, UMNPC president Kara Ayotte said the UMNPC wanted to support other labor unions who share similar values to them.

“We stand united in solidarity against the one employer, and we all deserve dignity, fair share wages and human decency and to push back on social justice issues and things like that,” Ayotte said. “(We are continuing) the tireless fight to preserve our contract and provide patients safe patient care at the same time against an employer that is always looking to sacrifice that and in the name of wages and things like that.”

column3: Santa Ono sits down with The Daily to talk student activism, canceled CSG resolutions
column7:
The University Insider is The Daily’s first faculty and staff-oriented newsletter. This weekly newsletter will give U-M faculty and staff the ability to see the most important issues on campus and in Ann Arbor — particularly those related to administrative decisions — from the perspective of an independent news organization. It will also provide a better understanding of student perspectives.








The Michigan Daily sat down with University President Santa Ono Tuesday afternoon for the first time this semester to discuss campus activism regarding divestment, former Michigan football head coach Jim Harbaugh’s decision to end his tenure after the season concluded and new campus free speech principles. The Daily provided Ono with the questions prior to the interview. This interview has been edited and condensed for clarity.

The Michigan Daily: Throughout the past academic year, there has been a lot of activism on campus in relation to the University of Michigan’s investment portfolio. Hundreds of students have called for the University to divest from Israeli companies that are involved with the ongoing war in Gaza. How has University administration discussed divestment amid growing campus activism?

Santa Ono: There are investment — or divestment — decisions at the level of the Board of Regents. I’m a nonvoting member of the Board of Regents, so we certainly have discussed it several times, even relatively recently, because there was a board meeting just last week. As you probably know, the Board of Regents has stuck with their position that they typically do not invest — they look at overall performance. That is the decision that we have discussed a number of times. We should discuss it because when you have students who are making their concerns heard, it’s important for us to take that into account. So we certainly have, as a consequence, discussed this. 

TMD: During South African apartheid, the University eventually decided to divest from all holdings in South Africa. Has the University considered taking similar action in regard to its investment in companies that fund the Israeli military?

SO: That has been discussed several times, and the decision of the Regents has been to not divest.

TMD: On Nov. 17, 40 U-M students were arrested at a pro-Palestine protest inside the Alexander G. Ruthven Building. Since then, students have protested and petitioned for the criminal charges against those arrested to be dropped. Did you make the decision to arrest these students?  

SO: What happened was the Division of Public Safety and Security was here on site as well as supporting units, and so decisions were made on-site at the time. So what I’ve been told — I wasn’t there — is that it was the individuals who were actually in the suite and in the building who were asked multiple times at 7:30 p.m. to leave because the closing time for Ruthven was 5 p.m. were arrested. Many of the students actually did leave, but those who stayed after being warned multiple times were arrested and asked to leave the building, then released once they were outside. So the decision was made locally at the time, as usually is the case. 

TMD: The University canceled the election for a pair of CSG resolutions in relation to Israel and Palestine this past semester: propositions AR 13-025, calling to “ask the University to recognize the millions of people undergoing genocide in Gaza,” and AR 13-026, which “requests that the University and its Regents continue to lend support to all students impacted by ongoing violence in Israel and Gaza.” How do you respond to the cancellation of the voting of those particular resolutions, and student allegations that the University did not want to acknowledge the violence in Gaza in that way?

SO: That’s certainly not the case. We have mentioned the violence in Gaza multiple times in my written statements. I acknowledge today in front of you that I’m saddened and concerned about the violence in the Middle East. In terms of the resolutions, you’ve seen my statement about the rationale for that. You also know the timeline of what happened. We wanted the election to move forward — it came across twice. The first time as you know, the vote was canceled administratively by our General Counsel Tim Lynch because an email was sent through the Registrar’s Office that was supportive of one of the resolutions. As he articulated in his statement, the integrity of the election in the view of the administration was affected by that. And then the resolution came around again, for a vote. 

As for the reason for canceling the second time — we certainly typically support freedom of expression and freedom of speech — but as you have reported, the climate on campus reached a pitch that was concerning to the safety of many members of the community. We know that because of the communications that we receive, because of our conversations with DPSS as well as national security organizations themselves. My top priority is the safety of this community. So students on both sides of the debate regarding resolutions, their safety was my top priority. And, as I said in my statement in canceling the resolutions, that was the reason that we stepped in. We would support it if it wasn’t a safety concern.

TMD: The University of Michigan chapter of Alpha Epsilon Pi was suspended from the national organization over an alleged hazing incident. Does the University plan to investigate these incidents? If so, how?

SO: That particular fraternity is not a registered member of our Interfraternity Council, but the University did reach out to the national headquarters of that fraternity and also to the Ann Arbor Police Department once we heard about that. Hazing is something that we take very seriously. Even though that fraternity is not officially part of our Interfraternity Council, they’re still our students. We did get involved because of the safety of our students. I can’t talk about the investigation because it is ongoing.

TMD: The University’s Residential Advisors plan to vote to unionize by late February, an action which would be recognized under a resolution passed by the Board of Regents in June 2020. The RAs plan to demand for higher pay for Residential Staff coordinators and free laundry for residents. They also plan to demand for the establishment of a legal support system to protect RAs who have been harassed by Michigan Housing or residents. Do you support the unionization of the RAs, and how will the University respond to demands for pay and protection?

SO: So this is once again a potential policy that supports the right of any group to unionize. The same policy recommends that I as president remain neutral. So to answer your question, I cannot speak to that, but as an institution, we support the right to unionize.

TMD: Michigan Housing also promised to operate a Residential Experience Council to connect the RAs and the University administration, but that council is not currently being utilized and now focuses on the issues of residents instead of RAs. How do you plan to address the lack of connection between RAs and the administration?

SO: This is actually something quite personal to me because I was in the Residence Hall Association at the University of Chicago. When I was an undergraduate, I was an advisor, an RA and an orientation aide, and I know how much hard work they do for student life in general and the quality of the residential experience. I understand that I could be wrong, but the Residential Experience Council was discontinued because of a lack of a critical mass of attendance before my arrival at the institution. I don’t really know why it doesn’t exist currently, but I certainly will look into it. I support the concept of having that line of communication because of the contributions that they’ve made to the residential experience.

TMD: We wanted to ask about an incident in November 2023 when Carin Ehrenberg, a School of Information external advisory board member, attended a protest and verbally assaulted Arab and Muslim students. Does the University have any plans to address this situation?

SO: I don’t really know the full timeline of what happened, but my understanding is that the interim dean at the School of Information has made a decision. So because that’s a local decision regarding that school, and the individual you’re talking about has an advisory role in that school, I recommend talking to the dean. We usually don’t make local decisions at the University level.

TMD: As a follow-up to that, students have voiced concerns over Islamophobia being an ongoing problem at the University across all schools and levels. Is this something that you at the University level are working to address?

SO: We had a number of students who were doxxed, and those are Islamophobic acts that are antithetical to our values as an institution. I’ve had a chance to meet with those students and to provide support for those students. I am very appreciative of the time they spent advising me, and it will remain a priority moving forward. The well-being of all of our students is a top priority for the institution. It is important for us to continue to advocate for all students, including those of Muslim origin.

TMD: When you started your tenure at the University, you expressed a commitment to being more engaged with student organizations and the campus community than previous leadership. How specifically have you engaged with the campus community this semester?

SO: It would take a very long time to talk about all the things I’ve done. We spoke with two of the students from Students Allied for Freedom and Equality, and they were very appreciative of that interaction. I’ve spoken with students in a variety of academic settings. I interacted with members of a class from LSA just a few days ago. There was a Henry Russell Lecture yesterday with undergraduate and graduate students, and I had a chance to interact with students there. The musical theater program actually asked me to be a guest speaker for their production of “The 25th Annual Putnam County Spelling Bee.” I go to many, many sporting events and try to support our scholar-athletes — not only as they compete, but also in talking to them. One of the most important ways is an entirely new mechanism for me to interact not only with students but also with the faculty, called the President’s Council. We’ve already met twice. We’re going to have a third meeting, and the Council has student representation – both undergraduate and graduate from all three campuses. I find that to be a very impactful, meaningful way to communicate. The CSG president and vice president sit on that council, so I consulted with them about policies and fun things as well. One of my joys is to be able to interact with students in a variety of ways.

TMD: What were the contract talks like with former Michigan football coach Jim Harbaugh before he took the job with the Los Angeles Chargers? How close were you to reaching a deal?

SO: These are human resources issues. I can’t really talk about how they went, but this is probably widely known: I really respect Coach Harbaugh. I like him as a person, I consider him a friend. So I can say the entire institution really tried our level best to retain him. We wish him the best with the Chargers. He brought us to the National Championship. We haven’t had one for some time, and so all I can say is that we would have loved for him to stay, but we wish him the best. It really is up to him as the person making a decision about whether to stay or go to the NFL, so I respect that personal choice.

TMD: The Lecturers’ Employee Organization began their contract negotiations in October 2023 and is currently bargaining for increased professor development grants and the ability to apply for grants, help in recruiting faculty in the Computer Science Department and raises for new hires and long-serving lecturers. How does the University plan to negotiate with LEO on these demands?

SO: Certainly I am briefed about those negotiations, so I know they are meeting on a regular basis. I think that LEO has contributed enormously to the mission of the University. They’re just in the midst of the bargaining — it’s an iterative process. We really value lecturers, and I hope that they can come to an agreement soon on all of those different economic and noneconomic terms. I’m optimistic. I understand that progress is being made, and I hope that there’s a mutually satisfactory contract that is agreed upon sooner rather than later.

TMD: How involved were you in the talks to hire Sherrone Moore as a new football head coach? 

SO: I would say that is more the responsibility of our athletic director, Warde Manuel. He did a great job after Coach Harbaugh made his decision to go to the NFL. Manuel really sprang into action very quickly. I had a chance to speak with the candidate prior to this being named. I’m thrilled with that choice, and I’m thrilled with what Sherrone Moore has done in a very short time for an outstanding coaching team. I know that he’s very active in recruiting scholar-athletes as well. 

TMD: The Board of Regents recently approved a new set of free speech principles after a series of protests and shows of student activism on campus. What are your goals for how they will impact student activism on campus moving forward?

SO: So the first thing I will say is they weren’t created in response to activism or protests. We’ve been working on them for more than a year, and they were one of the very first objectives of my presidency. We have been going through a process of conversations with the Faculty Senate and Senate Advisory Committee on University Affairs, the committee of faculty led by General Counsel Tim Lynch, so it’s just been ongoing. This is something that is not a top-down policy, or something that has been informed through those conversations with faculty. We are assembling a committee with faculty, staff and students to look at the recently approved general statement and ask how can we do better in terms of supporting freedom of speech and diversity of thought, that certainly, all institutions can do better.

TMD: This year, the U-M chapter of United Students Against Sweatshops sent two letters — one in March and a follow-up in November — about the University’s continued partnership with Nike after the company’s alleged wage theft in Thailand. You responded in October saying you were committed to a just resolution. How has University administration been working towards achieving that?

SO: We have an advisory committee — a member of that student group is actually a part of the committee and helped shape the decision. We have written to Nike, expressing our concerns, as was the recommendation of the committee advisory to me. And so we’re waiting on that response. To my knowledge, we haven’t heard back from Nike yet. So I greatly appreciate the students’ activism, the advice from the advisory committee and the action that we took.

TMD: As part of a Daily tradition, we ask a fun question at the end of each interview with the University president. What is your favorite Ann Arbor “sweet treat?”

SO: Donuts at Washtenaw Dairy.

column3: Board of Regents holds meeting on protection for faculty, free speech principles
column7:
The University Insider is The Daily’s first faculty and staff-oriented newsletter. This weekly newsletter will give U-M faculty and staff the ability to see the most important issues on campus and in Ann Arbor — particularly those related to administrative decisions — from the perspective of an independent news organization. It will also provide a better understanding of student perspectives.








The University of Michigan Board of Regents met in University Hall at the Alexander G. Ruthven Building for a meeting Thursday afternoon to discuss their newly enacted free speech principles and review the administration’s work over the past few months. 

University President Santa Ono opened the meeting by addressing the University community about the Michigan football team’s recent national championship win and the expansion of generative artificial intelligence at the University.

“We are continuing to expand our efforts in generative AI specifically through our world-class faculty at the Center for Academic Innovation,” Ono said. “We are developing 35 online courses which will empower learners to build a central new skill.”

The main focus of the meeting was freedom of speech following the University’s new freedom of speech guidelines approved in January. University Provost Laurie McCauley announced the creation of a set of resources for faculty members who experience harassment due to their research or academic work. 

“In addition to the harm that threats and harassment cause our faculty as individuals, such behavior, often deliberately, has the potential to compromise our academic mission,” McCauley said. “The University of Michigan is committed to offering the breadth of its resources and support to faculty experiencing threats and/or harassment.”

During her student government report, Meera Herle, Central Student Government President, expressed her frustration with the University’s treatment of student activists in recent months.

“As a current student, I have recently been disappointed,” Herle said. “I feel that our institution is falling short in the last few months in making students feel safe to express disagreement with others and encouraging respectful and kind dialogue across differences. When students are being doxxed for expressing their identity and viewpoints and when peaceful protests are met with vitriol from entities outside of this university, I find myself frustrated by the administration’s lack of communication with and support for student leaders.”

Later in her address, Herle said while some students may feel uncomfortable due to the presence of protests, she wanted to make sure administration decisions are not based on the opinions of a minority of students.

“I found that students have difficulties distinguishing between what makes them feel uncomfortable and what makes them feel unsafe,” Herle said. “I think this is an area where education is needed to encourage our students to think independently.”

Domenico Grasso, chancellor of University of Michigan-Dearborn, announced that the Engineering Lab Building will be renamed after former Dean Anthony England who previously worked for NASA, including assisting in returning astronauts during the Apollo 13 mission.

“(England) has equally left his mark on the creative and innovative modes of education and research inside the building,” Grasso said. “I am proud to recognize Professor England’s dedication to U-M Dearborn — and to celebrate the many students’ lives he has helped transform — by naming our Engineering Lab Building in his honor.”

During the public comments portion of the meeting, several speakers urged the board to divest from companies profiting from Israel’s military campaign in Gaza.

Rackham student Nathan Kim spoke as a member of the TAHRIR Coalition, a group of student organizations dedicated to the liberation of Palestine. Kim said he was disappointed in the University’s continued investment in these companies.

“It is difficult for me to grasp how the University could knowingly make investments that work against its stated ideals,” Kim said. “I want to compel the Regents to act — I’m sure at the very least, all of you will agree to a transparency and accountability initiative.”

As the board and members of the U-M administration left the meeting, pro-Palestine protesters blocked the exit to the parking lot, prompting an increased police response and aggression toward student protestors.

Herle closed out her speech by calling on the University to support student activists by giving them a platform.

“It is my and many of my fellow students’ opinion that the University has a long way to go in meeting conflict and controversy with understanding and reason and refuting our opponents rather than refusing them a platform,” Herle said. “I hope we can all strive to better employ these pillars of productive dialogue.”

“It is difficult for me to grasp how the University could knowingly make investments that work against its stated ideals,” <PERSON> said. “I want to compel the Regents to act — I’m sure at the very least, all of you will agree to a transparency and accountability initiative.”

As the board and members of the U-M administration left the meeting, pro-Palestine protesters blocked the exit to the parking lot, prompting an increased police response and aggression toward student protestors.

<PERSON> closed out her speech by calling on the University to support student activists by giving them a platform.

“It is my and many of my fellow students’ opinion that the University has a long way to go in meeting conflict and controversy with understanding and reason and refuting our opponents rather than refusing them a platform,” <PERSON> said. “I hope we can all strive to better employ these pillars of productive dialogue.”

column3: Students react to U-M Board of Regents newly-adopted free speech principles
column7:
The University Insider is The Daily’s first faculty and staff-oriented newsletter. This weekly newsletter will give U-M faculty and staff the ability to see the most important issues on campus and in Ann Arbor — particularly those related to administrative decisions — from the perspective of an independent news organization. It will also provide a better understanding of student perspectives.








The University of Michigan Board of Regents unanimously adopted a new set of Principles on Diversity of Thought and Freedom of Expression on Jan. 16. The principles, which follow numerous student-led protests against the Israeli military violence in Gaza, reiterated the University’s commitment to open dialogue and free speech on campus.

The principles were originally drafted in October 2023 to build upon prior policies approved by the Board of Regents, including the 1977 Freedom of Speech Guidelines and the 1988 Freedom of Speech and Artistic Expression policy. The principles highlight that protection of speech does not extend to appropriate or civil conduct or speech that breaches any law or U-M policy.

The approval of the principles comes after the cancellation of Central Student Government elections for ballot initiatives AR 13-025 and AR 13-026. After the U-M administration authorized and approved an email encouraging students to vote yes on AR 13-025 and no on AR 13-026, two students were doxxed and accused of stealing the student body’s emails to send the email.

The American Civil Liberties Union of Michigan addressed a letter to University President Santa Ono and Timothy Lynch, the University’s vice president and general counsel in December. In the letter, the ACLU expressed concerns over the suppression of student speech and advocacy regarding the crisis in Palestine and Israel. The letter specifically highlights the canceled elections, the shutting down of student email listservs, the removal of posters showing support for Palestine from graduate students’ office windows and the University’s response to a campus sit-in and protest as examples of this pattern.

In an interview with The Michigan Daily, Om Shah, co-chair of the U-M American Civil Liberties Union Undergraduate Chapter, said if the University truly prioritized free speech on campus, they would not have canceled voting on the CSG ballot initiatives.

“This really just seems like a move by the University to avoid any negative attention, which I think is completely silly and pointless,” Shah said. “The University really hasn’t addressed that other than issuing the statement which doesn’t even mention the letter, mention the students that were arrested, mention the elections that were canceled. If the University really cared about free speech, they would let a democratic election go on and they wouldn’t cancel it and perpetuate Islamophobic narratives.”

Engineering senior Zaynab Elkolaly, director of activism for Students Allied for Freedom and Equality, said in an interview with The Daily she believes the principles are an empty statement.

“When they said ‘we strive to meet conflict and controversy with understanding and reason, refuting our opponents rather than revoking invitations or refusing them a platform, and contesting their ideas instead of attacking their character,’ what the values they’re claiming to be proponents of have not been displayed on this campus at all,” Elkolaly said.

Elkolaly said she and other students advocating for Palestine want to see a statement from the University that recognizes the Israeli military violence in Palestine as genocide and condemns Islamaphobic rhetoric on campus.

“What we’d like to see from the administration, first of all, obviously, (is) complete condemnation of canceling the vote and destroying students’ democracy,” Elkolaly said. “But also a statement which directly identifies the Israeli military regime on Palestine as a genocide, apartheid and ethnic cleansing … (and) explicitly condemning the rise of Islamophobia, anti-Arab and anti-Palestinian sentiments on our campus.”

Rackham student Ekaterina Olson Shipyatsky, co-chair of U-M student organization Jewish Voice for Peace, said the TAHRIR coalition has still not been able to speak to Ono regarding divestment from Israel.

“The (TAHRIR coalition) of over 60 student orgs that has been demanding to meet with President Ono for the last months still has no word about any kind of meeting with him to talk about divestment,” Shipyatsky said. “The 40 students who were arrested in Ruthven are still banned from Ruthven, which means that they’re still banned from attending regents meetings.”

Shipyatsky told The Daily she feels the principles will not truly protect U-M students advocating for Palestine.

“There’s still this whole ongoing police investigation into students and the threat of more police repression against students who were in Ruthven on (Nov.) 17th,” Shipyatsky said. “So against that backdrop, it feels really hard to believe that these free speech principles were adopted to protect us, to protect students who are fighting and fighting to get a conversation going about the ongoing genocide in Palestine.”

In an email to The Daily, University spokesperson Colleen Mastony wrote that the purpose of the principles is to reaffirm the University’s policy and beliefs on free speech.

“In times of social conflict and strained discourse, a commitment to free expression is especially important,” Mastony wrote. “Our community is increasingly grappling with controversial topics, and these principles help to reiterate what we believe.”

Shah said the principles will not distract students from calling for divestment and advocating for Palestinian liberation. 

“These are all just distractions that the University is trying to use at the end of the day to make us forget about our original campaign and what we’re fighting for,” Shah said. “Whatever we’re going through on campus is nothing compared to what Palestinians on the ground are going through, obviously, and we want to do our best to make sure that our university is not complicit in the genocide.”

column3: ‘The fight isn’t over’: UMich students take part in silent pro-Palestine demonstration at Senate Assembly
column7:
The University Insider is The Daily’s first faculty and staff-oriented newsletter. This weekly newsletter will give U-M faculty and staff the ability to see the most important issues on campus and in Ann Arbor — particularly those related to administrative decisions — from the perspective of an independent news organization. It will also provide a better understanding of student perspectives.








More than 50 University of Michigan students filed into the Alexander G. Ruthven Building for a silent protest staged at the Senate Assembly meeting. At this meeting, faculty members voted on resolutions determining their response to the University’s cancellation of the U-M Central Student Government midterm ballot proposal and asking the University to divest from companies with financial ties to Israel.

The silent demonstration was organized by Students Allied for Freedom and Equality and the TAHRIR Coalition, a group of more than 60 U-M student organizations, including SAFE, advocating for the University’s divestment from companies with financial ties to Israel.

Senate Assembly members voted on two resolutions at the meeting. One condemned the University’s decision to cancel voting on two CSG resolutions regarding the University’s position on the Israel-Hamas war — AR 13-025 and AR 13-026 — after the University determined that an email sent to all U-M students urging them to vote yes on AR 13-025 and no on AR 13-026 violated University policy. The other resolution called on the University to divest from companies that profit from ongoing Israeli military violence.

About 10 students waited outside the Ruthven Building because they were banned from entering the building for one year after the Nov. 17 protest at the building, where 40 students were arrested for trespassing.

Zaynab Elkolaly, SAFE director of activism, stood outside the Ruthven Building during the silent demonstration. In an interview with The Michigan Daily, she said she stayed outside to support other students from afar. 

“Our peers are inside flooding the room to send our message on our behalf here today at this specific faculty senate,” Elkolaly said. “(It) has a huge pull on administration and an affirmation that they’re calling for divestment would be (a) huge benefit to the cause.”

Alex Sepulveda, activism chair for the U-M chapter of Jewish Voice for Peace, also waited outside Ruthven due to the ban. In an interview with The Daily before the event, Sepulveda said he came to support U-M faculty members.

“I’m here to join the chorus of people at this campus,” Sepulveda said. “I’d say that we are supporting our faculty as they demand from the University administration that we inquire into the ethicality of our investments…. The Board of Regents has absolutely zero intention of divesting because they’re beholden to corporations — not beholden to students. They have demonstrated absolutely zero intentions to make any sort of legitimate relationship with student organizers here on campus.”

At the start of the meeting, attendees who were unable to find a seat were asked to leave the meeting. 

Public Health senior Rawan Antar waited on the first floor of Ruthven because there wasn’t enough seating in the room. Antan told The Daily she felt frustrated with the lack of accommodations. 

“We were sitting alongside the (students) on the floor, and they told us that there is only space for as many seats as there were, which was like 200 that were set up,” Antan said. “ … It was filled up by the time I was there.”

In an email to The Daily, University spokesperson Kim Broekhuizen said the U-M administration respects student protests and that spaces for protestors opened up as the meeting progressed.

“We have great respect for peaceful demonstrations, which has been an important part of our campus for almost as long as we have existed,” Broekhuizen wrote. “U-M students care about their communities and the world we all live in and they are not shy about advocating for what they believe in. The room hit capacity, but as guests left and spots opened — others were allowed to enter the room.”

The first resolution the Senate Assembly voted on condemned the administration’s cancellation of the CSG midterm ballot. The second called on the Board of Regents to divest from companies invested in Israel. The first resolution passed with 38 in favor, 17 against and 5 abstaining, and the second passed with 37 yes, 15 no and 5 abstaining. 

In an email to The Daily, Silvia Pedraza, professor of sociology and American culture and Senate Advisory Committee on University Affairs member, wrote that she believes the resolution came from faculty members seeing the ongoing violence in Gaza and wanting to take action to end the University’s support for it. .

“I believe it came out of the distress faculty feel at seeing so many Palestinians in Gaza killed, their families destroyed, their bodies maimed,” Pedraza wrote. “The faculty really do not know the financial implications of that vote, and that is up to our financial specialists at U of M and the leadership of the University to assess. But the faculty vote indicates they want the leadership of the University not to collaborate with the enormous harm the people in Gaza are enduring.”

After hearing that the vote passed, Elkolaly told The Daily she felt excited about the outcome but recognized the importance of continuing to advocate for divestment.

“We’re incredibly elated,” Elkolaly said. “We’re glad that the Faculty Senate was able to look beyond the garbage bureaucratic measures that opponents were using to hinder the vote. Of course, the fight isn’t over. The Faculty Senate only has the capacity to make recommendations to the Regents. This resolution being passed does not materially change (the University’s) investments, so the fight is with the Regents. We invite everyone who’d like to see divestment progress to the next level to join us.”

column3: MSA National releases statement  in solidarity with UMich SAFE and MSA
column7:
The University Insider is The Daily’s first faculty and staff-oriented newsletter. This weekly newsletter will give U-M faculty and staff the ability to see the most important issues on campus and in Ann Arbor — particularly those related to administrative decisions — from the perspective of an independent news organization. It will also provide a better understanding of student perspectives.








The Muslim Students Association of the U.S. & Canada released a statement on Jan. 12 in solidarity with University of Michigan student organizations Students Allied for Freedom and Equality and Muslim Students’ Association. The press release cites multiple instances of targeted harassment against students who have expressed pro-Palestine views, and included three recommendations for U-M administration to create a safe environment for Muslim, Arab and Palestinian students on campus.

The statement referenced the Oct. 13 verbal assault of Arab and Muslim students by Carin Ehrenberg, a School of Information board member. The students had been attending a peaceful sit-in protest outside University President Santa Ono’s house in response to his Oct. 10 statement on the Israel-Hamas war, which did not include any mentions of Palestine. In a video posted to X that has since been taken down, Ehrenberg can be seen asking one student at the protest “Are you going to send one of your terrorists after us?” and shouted “rapists and murderers” at others. In the release, MSA National wrote that Ono’s statement disregarded the violence in Palestine and the experiences of Arab and Muslim students, and expressed disappointment in the Information School’s decision to not take action against Ehrenberg.

MSA National’s statement also mentioned that 40 U-M students were arrested in the Alexander G. Ruthven Building on Nov. 17 when protesting the University’s investment in companies with financial ties to Israel. In response to the protest, the University called in police from more than 10 departments who denied protesters access to water and restrooms. According to MSA National, one protester fainted, and a police officer ripped off a Muslim woman’s hijab.

According to MSA National’s statement, a Central Student Government member proposed a resolution on Oct. 20 to ask the University to reconsider its position on the Israel-Hamas war. The resolution, which received more than 1,000 signatures, was rejected by the administration due to “issues with the resolution’s wording.” Following the rejection of the resolution, two other resolutions, AR 13-025 and AR 13-026, went to a campus-wide vote during the CSG midterm elections in November. 

AR 13-025 requests that the University takes an educated and non-discriminatory stance on violence and systems of apartheid in light of the Israel-Hamas war and AR 13-026, which focused on providing support for those impacted by the war. The University decided to cancel the vote on these resolutions, claiming it violated CSG election integrity and the U-M Standard Practice Guide. 

On Nov. 29 in a now-deleted post on X, two students engaged in pro-Palestine advocacy on campus were falsely accused of stealing U-M student emails to send a message to all undergraduates asking them to vote yes on AR 13-025 and no on AR 13-026. The email, which was sent by a graduate student, was authorized by an employee at the Office of Registrar to be sent through the Targeted Email service. 

The statement included three recommendations for University administration: to issue a statement in support of Muslim and Arab students who have experienced harassment, to issue another statement explaining why the two CSG resolutions on the ballot were canceled and to arrange a meeting between administrators and students to discuss concerns related to the University’s past actions. 

In an email to The Michigan Daily, University spokesperson Kim Broekhuizen said the administration has seen MSA National’s statement. Citing previous University statements and a letter from Ono, she said the University will continue to monitor campus tensions and speak to student leaders. 

“We are aware of the National Muslim Student Association’s statement in support of U-M’s MSA and SAFE,” Broekhuizen said. “We will review the suggestions and consider the appropriate action. … The university’s executive leaders continue to engage with students to address campus climate concerns.” 

In an email to The Daily, SAFE president Salma Hamamy wrote she expected the University to continue to receive national attention regarding its suppression of student advocacy. 

“While there has been extensive advocacy work across hundreds of campuses, the statement of solidarity from the national board of the Muslim Students Association was meant to highlight that there is an extensive issue of repression at the University of Michigan that has drawn out national concern,” Hamamy said. “This statement covers issues of police violence, doxxing, harassment, canceling of student-led democratic procedures and everything in between from the fall semester. So long as these issues remain prevalent, national eyes will remain focused on our university.”
